Case Study 1: NHS Trust — The Systematic Segregation Failure

Background

Midlands Community Health NHS Trust (composite) provides community nursing, district nursing, and minor treatment services across 18 community sites — GP-attached clinics, health centres, and a small community hospital. It generates approximately 45 tonnes of clinical waste per month, split between infectious waste (18 01 03*, yellow bags), offensive/hygiene waste (18 01 04, orange bags), sharps (18 01 03*, yellow-lidded containers), and pharmaceutical waste (18 01 09, blue containers).

In 2022, a waste treatment contractor conducting routine visual inspection of incoming loads from the trust noticed a pattern: orange bags labelled as "offensive/hygiene waste" (non-hazardous, 18 01 04) frequently contained items that should have been in yellow bags — used gloves with visible blood contamination, used wound dressings from infected patients, and on one occasion, a needle protruding through the bag material. The contractor flagged this to their compliance team, who reported it to the Environment Agency.

What Happened

The Environment Agency initiated an investigation. They reviewed 18 months of consignment notes and waste transfer notes. They found that the waste descriptions on the WTNs systematically categorised a proportion of infectious waste as offensive/hygiene waste (18 01 04 rather than 18 01 03*). This had two immediate effects: (1) it reduced the trust's disposal costs significantly, since offensive/hygiene waste is cheaper to dispose of than hazardous infectious waste; and (2) it meant the trust was not completing consignment notes (required for 18 01 03* as hazardous waste) for a significant portion of its infectious waste.

EA inspectors visited 11 of the 18 sites. They found that several sites had no current training records for waste handling for their staff. At three sites, orange bags and yellow bags were stored in the same unlocked external area, accessible without a key. At one site, the orange bags were substantially cheaper to procure than yellow bags and had been ordered exclusively because of a budget decision made by the facilities manager without waste management input.

The investigation also revealed that the trust's waste management policy had not been updated since 2018 and did not reference the Controlled Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2012 or the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005 in its operational sections.

Legislation Breached

  • s.34 EPA 1990 — Duty of Care breach: inaccurate written description of waste on WTNs (infectious waste described as non-infectious); failure to complete consignment notes for hazardous (18 01 03*) waste movements.
  • Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005, reg.16 — Failure to produce required consignment notes for hazardous waste.
  • Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005, reg.19 — Mixing infectious waste (18 01 03*) with non-hazardous offensive waste (18 01 04) in orange bags.
  • HSE dimension: Lack of training records for waste handlers constituted a breach of the Health and Safety at Work Act 1974 s.2(2)(c) (provision of instruction, training, and supervision).

What Should Have Happened

This failure was not caused by a single rogue act. It developed over time through a combination of: (a) cost pressures creating an incentive for mis-segregation; (b) insufficient training meaning front-line staff did not understand the difference between 18 01 03* and 18 01 04; (c) a waste management policy that was outdated and not embedded in operational practice; and (d) no internal audit mechanism to detect segregation failures.

☐Prevention Step 1: Annual waste management policy review referencing current legislation, conducted by someone with waste management competency (not just the facilities manager).
☐Prevention Step 2: Site-specific waste training for all clinical and non-clinical staff who handle waste, with documented records — ideally refreshed annually.
☐Prevention Step 3: Regular internal audits (quarterly) of waste segregation at point of generation, with findings reported to a named compliance officer or board-level sponsor.
☐Prevention Step 4: Any decision to change waste container procurement (bag colour, size, supplier) should be reviewed by the waste manager for regulatory implications before implementation.
☐Prevention Step 5: A system for receiving and acting on feedback from the waste contractor — treatment facilities often detect segregation failures before any regulator does.

The Outcome

The Environment Agency issued a formal enforcement notice requiring the trust to implement a comprehensive corrective action plan within 90 days. The notice specified: updated waste management policy, mandatory training with recorded completion for all staff, installation of locked storage at all 18 sites, and quarterly internal audits reported to the trust board.

The EA also pursued prosecution for the consignment note failures. The trust pleaded guilty at Magistrates' Court and received a fine of £78,000. Taking into account the trust's early guilty plea and the corrective actions already implemented, no custodial sentences were sought against individuals. However, the trust's director of estates was the subject of a formal s.157 EPA 1990 investigation (individual liability). The investigation was ultimately discontinued, but the process cost the individual concerned significant personal legal fees and several months of acute professional stress.

The CQC was notified by the EA. The subsequent CQC inspection rated the trust's "safe" domain as "Requires Improvement" in relation to waste management — a rating that appeared in the public inspection report and generated local media coverage.

Student Analysis Exercise 1

Having read Case Study 1, consider the following questions before we move on:

  1. The trust's cost-saving decision — ordering cheaper orange bags — was made by a facilities manager without waste management input. What governance structures could prevent this kind of decision being made in isolation?
  2. The waste treatment contractor noticed the problem and reported it. What does this tell us about the role of waste contractors in the compliance ecosystem? Should organisations actively incentivise contractors to report concerns?
  3. The EA investigated 18 months of records. How far back do your organisation's waste transfer notes and consignment notes actually go? Would they survive 18 months of scrutiny?

Case Study 2: Dental Practice — Carrier Registration and Documentation Failures

Background

Brightside Dental Practice (composite) is a mid-sized, multi-dentist practice in a large English town, treating NHS and private patients. It generates clinical waste including sharps (18 01 03*), amalgam (18 01 10*), non-cytotoxic medicinal waste (18 01 09), and soft clinical waste. Monthly waste arisings are modest — approximately 40-50 kg of clinical waste plus amalgam separator collections twice yearly.

In 2023, the Environment Agency conducted a targeted inspection programme of small healthcare providers following intelligence that a number of waste carriers were operating without valid registrations in the region. Brightside Dental was included in the programme following a routine check of registered waste producers in the area.

What Happened

An EA officer attended the practice unannounced and requested to see:

  • Waste transfer notes for the past 12 months
  • Consignment notes for hazardous waste (18 01 03*, 18 01 10*) for the past 3 years
  • Evidence that the waste carrier was a registered waste carrier
  • Evidence that the treatment/disposal facility held an appropriate environmental permit

The practice manager produced a folder of documents. The EA officer found:

  1. No consignment notes at all. The practice had been using waste transfer notes only — not understanding that its sharps and amalgam waste were hazardous and required the consignment note regime. The practice had been generating hazardous waste without consignment notes for at least 4 years.
  2. Waste transfer notes incomplete. The WTNs that did exist lacked EWC codes, the carrier's registration number was absent from 7 of the 12 most recent notes, and two WTNs were signed only by the carrier — not by the practice.
  3. The carrier's registration had lapsed 14 months previously. The practice had never checked. The carrier — a small local company — had simply continued to operate after its registration expired. The EA check of the Public Register confirmed the registration had lapsed.
  4. The amalgam waste had been collected by a person who was not registered as a specialist waste carrier for hazardous waste. Amalgam waste (18 01 10*) has additional requirements as a mercury-containing hazardous waste — the carrier must be specifically registered for hazardous waste.

Legislation Breached

  • Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005, reg.16 and reg.22 — Failure to complete consignment notes for hazardous waste movements (18 01 03* sharps, 18 01 10* amalgam) over a 4-year period. This was a systematic, long-standing failure.
  • s.34 EPA 1990 (Duty of Care) — Incomplete waste transfer notes (missing EWC codes, missing carrier registration number, missing signatures); transfer of waste to an unregistered carrier.
  • Control of Pollution (Amendment) Act 1989, s.1 — Knowingly using an unregistered carrier (the practice "knowingly used" the carrier in the sense that they continued to use them without checking registration).

The Outcome

The EA pursued a prosecution against both the dental practice (as a body corporate) and the practice principal (as the director with management responsibility for compliance under s.157 EPA 1990). The carrier was separately prosecuted for operating without registration.

At the Magistrates' Court:

  • The practice was fined £22,500 (reduced from a higher indicated fine on account of the practice's relatively small turnover and early guilty plea).
  • The practice principal received a personal fine of £4,000 and a formal caution. A custodial sentence was not pursued, but the court noted that the conduct had been going on for 4 years and could not be characterised as a single oversight.

The practice was required to implement a formal waste management procedure, conduct annual waste carrier registration checks and document them, and attend EA-approved training. The CQC was notified and conducted a follow-up inspection. The practice received a CQC warning notice relating to Regulation 12 (Safe Care and Treatment) — the first in the practice's history. It was remedied within the required timeframe and registration was not at risk, but the practice principal later described the experience as "the most stressful thing I have dealt with in 18 years of running this practice."

Student Analysis Exercise 2

Before reading Case Study 3, reflect on Case Study 2:

  1. The practice principal had no idea that sharps waste and amalgam waste required consignment notes rather than WTNs. This is a genuine knowledge gap that exists in many small healthcare providers. Whose responsibility is it to address this gap — the regulator, the trade association (BDA), the waste contractor, or the individual provider?
  2. The waste carrier allowed its registration to lapse and continued to operate. The dental practice bore regulatory consequences. Is this fair? What mechanism could the law provide to better protect good-faith producers from contractor failures?
  3. The practice principal received a personal fine under s.157 EPA 1990. Do you think personal fines are an effective deterrent for owner-operated small practices? What alternative approaches might be more effective in changing behaviour?

Case Study 3: Care Home — Needlestick Injury, HSE and CQC Action

Background

Elmwood Residential Care Home (composite) is a 48-bed residential and nursing home caring for elderly residents, some with multiple comorbidities requiring regular medication administration and wound care. It is regulated by the CQC and employs 35 care staff. Its clinical waste includes infectious soft waste, sharps from insulin administration and other injections, and non-cytotoxic pharmaceutical waste.

In October 2023, a healthcare assistant (HCA) — on only her third shift at the care home — was collecting waste from a resident's room when she reached into a waste bag to compress it and was punctured on the thumb by a used insulin needle. The needle had been placed loose into a standard waste bag rather than into the sharps bin located in the same room. The resident was insulin-dependent and the sharps bin was located on the windowsill, approximately 2 metres from the waste bin — within easy reach during normal medication administration.

What Happened

The injury was a serious sharps injury. Blood-borne virus (BBV) first aid procedures were followed — the wound was bled, washed, and the HCA taken to the local emergency department for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) assessment. The resident's BBV status was unknown; the resident had dementia and could not reliably consent to a test. Baseline HIV and Hepatitis B/C tests were taken from the HCA. The care home manager reported the incident to the health and safety lead and documented it in the accident book.

However — and this is where the compliance failure began — the manager did not report the incident to the HSE under RIDDOR. She was uncertain whether a sharps injury qualified as a "specified injury" under RIDDOR and decided not to report it "until we know more." The 10-day RIDDOR reporting window passed without a report being submitted.

Two weeks after the incident, the HCA — who had been receiving PEP treatment and was absent from work — contacted the HSE directly, concerned that the care home had not notified them. The HSE launched an investigation.

What the HSE Investigation Found

  • The sharps injury had not been RIDDOR-reported within the 10-day deadline — a separate offence from the incident itself.
  • The HCA had received no specific waste handling training before her first shift or during her induction. Her induction checklist showed she had been "talked through" waste management in a 15-minute session on her first day but there were no records of specific sharps handling training or instruction on what to do with sharps found in waste bags.
  • There was no documented risk assessment for sharps in waste bags — a foreseeable risk in a nursing home environment where patients with cognitive impairment may misplace sharps.
  • The care home had not implemented the requirement under the Health and Safety (Sharp Instruments in Healthcare) Regulations 2013 to investigate sharps injuries and implement preventive measures. In fact, there had been a previous near-miss involving a loose syringe in a waste bag 8 months earlier, which had been logged in the accident book but not formally investigated or risk-assessed.
  • The COSHH assessment for biological hazards in waste handling was 6 years old and made no reference to insulin needles or to the specific risks associated with dementia patients and sharps disposal.

Legislation Breached

  • RIDDOR 2013, reg.4 — Failure to report a sharps injury to the HSE within 10 days (the injury was a "specified injury" under Schedule 1, RIDDOR 2013).
  • Health and Safety (Sharp Instruments in Healthcare) Regulations 2013, reg.5 — Failure to investigate and respond to the sharps injury; failure to have a procedure for managing sharps injuries in place; failure to review practices following the previous near-miss.
  • COSHH Regulations 2002, reg.6 — Inadequate COSHH risk assessment for biological hazards in waste handling.
  • HSWA 1974, s.2(2)(c) — Failure to provide adequate information, instruction, and training to the HCA before she began handling waste.
  • EA dimension: Investigation of the waste bag in which the needle was found revealed it had been classified as 18 01 04 (non-hazardous offensive waste). Since the needle contaminated the bag with blood, the bag should have been 18 01 03* (hazardous). EA issued an FPN for waste mis-description.

The Outcome

The HSE issued an improvement notice requiring the care home to:

  • Undertake a comprehensive COSHH assessment for all waste handling activities, specifically addressing sharps risks in the context of cognitively impaired residents.
  • Implement mandatory waste and sharps handling training for all staff before they undertake any care duties, with documented records.
  • Develop a written procedure for investigating sharps injuries and reporting near-misses.
  • Implement a system for ensuring loose sharps are not placed in soft waste bags (this included reviewing the placement of sharps bins, introducing point-of-use disposal trays for insulin administration, and training care staff on "safe sharps culture").

The HSE also applied Fee for Intervention. The care home was charged HSE officer time for the investigation — approximately £8,400. This was in addition to the costs of implementing the improvement notice requirements.

The HSE did not pursue prosecution in this case, taking into account that the RIDDOR non-reporting appeared to be a genuine knowledge error rather than a deliberate decision, and that the care home cooperated fully once contacted. However, the HSE officer made clear that had there been a second RIDDOR failure or evidence of deliberate concealment, prosecution would have been pursued.

The CQC was notified. An unannounced inspection followed. The CQC found multiple waste-related concerns at the inspection: unlocked sharps bins in some residents' rooms, no current training records for approximately 40% of care staff on waste handling, and a waste management policy that did not address the specific risks of caring for residents with cognitive impairment. The home's overall "safe" domain rating was downgraded to "Requires Improvement" and a warning notice was issued. The registered manager was required to provide monthly evidence of progress against the CQC's requirements.

For the HCA involved, the BBV test results (taken at 6 weeks and 3 months) were ultimately negative. The PEP treatment caused significant side effects and she was absent from work for 6 weeks. She did not return to the care home.

Student Analysis Exercise 3

Case Study 3 is perhaps the most humanly significant of the three. Reflect on the following:

  1. The care home manager failed to report under RIDDOR because she was "uncertain" whether the injury qualified. How should organisations prepare their managers to make correct RIDDOR reporting decisions under time pressure, without always having access to specialist legal advice?
  2. The previous near-miss 8 months before was logged but not formally investigated or risk-assessed. Many organisations treat near-misses as "near successes" — something almost went wrong but didn't, so no action needed. What does this case demonstrate about the importance of near-miss investigation?
  3. The HCA was on her third shift. She received a 15-minute verbal induction on waste handling. Who bears moral — and legal — responsibility for her injury? Is "the employer" an adequate answer, or should we consider how the induction was designed, who approved it, and who made the decision that 15 minutes was sufficient?
  4. The resident with dementia placed a sharps needle in a waste bag. The resident cannot be held legally responsible. But the foreseeable risk of this happening — in a dementia care environment — was known. What does the concept of "foreseeable risk" require of care home operators in this context?

Cross-Case Themes: What Compliance Failures Have in Common

Looking across all three case studies, several common themes emerge. These themes recur in real enforcement cases, and recognising them in your own organisation is the first step to preventing a similar outcome.

ThemeCase 1Case 2Case 3
Outdated or inadequate policies✓ Policy not updated since 2018✓ No formal waste management procedure✓ COSHH assessment 6 years old
Training gaps or missing records✓ No training records at multiple sites—✓ No specific sharps handling training for new staff
Knowledge deficit at decision-making level✓ Facilities manager unaware of EWC implications of bag choice✓ Principal unaware consignment notes required for sharps/amalgam✓ Manager unaware of RIDDOR sharps reporting obligation
Failure to verify contractor compliance—✓ Carrier registration never checked—
Previous signals ignored✓ Segregation failures presumably evident before contractor flagged them—✓ Near-miss 8 months before not investigated
Cost pressure driving compliance shortcuts✓ Cheaper bags used to reduce costs——
Personal liability for individuals✓ Director of estates investigated under s.157✓ Practice principal prosecuted and fined—
The Common Thread: In every case, the enforcement action was not the result of a sudden catastrophic failure. It was the culmination of systems failures that had been building over time — outdated policies, missing training, unchecked assumptions, and unexamined shortcuts. The final triggering event (the contractor's report, the EA inspection, the needlestick) did not create the problem; it revealed it. This is why proactive audit, regular policy review, and a genuine compliance culture — not just paper compliance — are the only reliable protection.

Key Takeaways

  • Real enforcement cases involve multiple simultaneous legislative breaches across different regulatory frameworks — not single isolated offences.
  • Personal liability under s.157 EPA 1990 is real and actively pursued. Directors and managers are not insulated from prosecution by the corporate structure.
  • The most common proximate causes of enforcement action are: (a) knowledge deficits at management level; (b) absent or outdated policies; (c) missing training records; (d) unchecked contractor compliance; and (e) failure to investigate previous incidents or near-misses.
  • RIDDOR reporting deadlines are strict — 10 days for specified injuries. Non-reporting is an independent offence, separate from the underlying incident.
  • Cost-driven compliance shortcuts (cheaper bags, unverified carriers, missing consignment notes) consistently result in enforcement costs that far exceed the savings made.
  • CQC inspection of waste management is triggered by EA and HSE referrals — regulators share information. You cannot manage each regulator in a silo.
  • The best protection against enforcement action is a genuine, embedded compliance culture — not compliance as a performance staged for inspections.

Reflection Questions

  1. All three case studies involved healthcare providers who were not deliberately trying to break the law. Does the law treat "honest mistakes" differently from deliberate non-compliance? Should it?
  2. In Case Study 1, the waste treatment contractor reported the problem. In Case Study 3, the injured worker reported to the HSE directly. What does this tell us about the limits of internal compliance systems, and the role of external parties (contractors, workers, the public) in driving regulatory accountability?
  3. Consider the financial outcomes across the three cases: £78,000 fine (Case 1), £26,500 total fines (Case 2), £8,400 FFI (Case 3) — plus in all cases the cost of remediation, legal advice, lost management time, and reputational damage. Using your knowledge of the organisations involved, would these costs have been justified as a waste management compliance investment over the preceding years? What does this tell us about the economics of compliance?
  4. Each of the three compliance failures was, in some sense, a failure of organisational culture as much as a failure of individual knowledge or action. What does a "compliance culture" in healthcare waste management actually look like at the operational level — not in a policy document, but in how people behave on a Tuesday morning in a care home, a dental surgery, or a community health centre?

quiz.q: Under EPA 1990 s.33, what is the maximum penalty for illegal deposit of controlled waste on Crown Court conviction?
options: Unlimited fine and/or up to 5 years imprisonment
quiz.explanation: EPA 1990 s.33 provides for an unlimited fine and/or up to 5 years imprisonment on conviction on indictment in Crown Court.

quiz.q: How many parts does a hazardous waste consignment note have?
quiz.explanation: A hazardous waste consignment note has 5 parts (A through E), each completed by different parties in the waste chain.

quiz.q: Which EWC chapter covers wastes from human healthcare?
quiz.explanation: EWC Chapter 18 covers wastes from human and animal healthcare and related research. 18 01 codes are human healthcare; 18 02 codes are animal healthcare.

quiz.q: The Duty of Care under EPA 1990 s.34 requires waste producers to:
options: Transfer waste only to authorised persons with a written description
Report all waste to CQC annually
quiz.explanation: The Duty of Care requires waste holders to prevent waste escaping, transfer only to authorised persons, provide an adequate written description, and complete waste transfer documentation.

quiz.q: Which regulatory body conducts healthcare provider inspections that include waste management as part of their quality framework?
quiz.explanation: CQC includes waste management in its inspection framework for regulated healthcare providers. EA focuses on environmental permitting and waste facilities.

quiz.q: Under the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005, hazardous waste records must be retained for a minimum of:
quiz.explanation: Hazardous waste records must be retained for a minimum of 3 years under the Hazardous Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2005.

quiz.q: What is the UN number used for clinical waste (unspecified) during road transport under ADR?
quiz.explanation: UN3291 is the classification for Clinical waste, unspecified, n.o.s. used for most categories of clinical waste during road transport.

quiz.q: A dental practice using an unregistered waste carrier to remove clinical waste would be committing an offence under:
options: Health and Safety at Work Act 1974
quiz.explanation: Using an unregistered carrier is a breach of the Duty of Care under EPA 1990 s.34, as waste must only be transferred to authorised persons.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a category of hazardous property under the EWC system?
quiz.explanation: There are 16 hazardous properties (HP1-HP16). HP17 does not exist. Radioactive waste is managed under separate regulations (Radioactive Substances Act / EPR 2016).

quiz.q: Environmental Permitting Regulations 2016 replaced:
options: Previous permitting frameworks with a consolidated system
quiz.explanation: EPR 2016 consolidated multiple permitting systems into a single framework for environmental permitting in England and Wales.

modules.title: Demystifying HTM 07-01
modules.description: A deep dive into Health Technical Memorandum 07-01 — the NHS's definitive guidance document for safe and sustainable management of healthcare waste. Understand every chapter, target, and practical requirement.
objectives: Describe the purpose, scope and structure of HTM 07-01 (2023 edition)
Explain the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy targets (20:20:60) and their implications
Apply the HTM 07-01 colour-coded waste segregation system correctly
Identify the roles of different healthcare professionals under HTM 07-01 guidance
Demonstrate understanding of the management approach requirements in Chapter 6

lessons.title: Overview: What Is HTM 07-01 and Why It Matters
lessons.content:
Welcome to Module 2: Demystifying HTM 07-01

Before we go any further, let's establish something important: HTM 07-01 is the single most influential document in UK healthcare waste management. Whether you work on a hospital ward, manage an NHS estate, run a GP surgery, or advise a dental practice, this document shapes everything you do with waste — from the bag you reach for in a clinical room to the contract your organisation signs with a waste disposal contractor. In this lesson, we're going to pull it apart so you understand exactly what it is, where it came from, and why the 2023 edition represents such a significant shift in how the NHS thinks about waste.

Learning Objectives
  • Understand the history and evolution of HTM 07-01 from 2006 to 2023
  • Know precisely what HTM 07-01 is — and what it is not
  • Understand the scope: which organisations it applies to
  • Appreciate the shift from compliance-only thinking to sustainability and circular economy
  • Connect HTM 07-01 to the NHS Net Zero programme

A Brief History: Three Editions That Shaped Healthcare Waste

HTM 07-01 did not appear fully formed in 2023. It has a history stretching back nearly two decades, and understanding that history explains why each successive edition looks the way it does.

The 2006 Edition: Getting the Basics Right

The first edition of Health Technical Memorandum 07-01 was published in 2006 by the Department of Health. At that time, the UK was in the early stages of standardising its approach to healthcare waste. The environment was one of growing regulatory complexity — the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005 had just come into force in England and Wales, the Special Waste Regulations applied in Scotland, and healthcare organisations were struggling to translate these legal obligations into day-to-day practice on the ward floor.

The 2006 edition was fundamentally about safety and compliance. It introduced the colour-coded waste segregation system that many of you will already be familiar with — yellow bags, orange bags, tiger stripe bags — and it provided the technical framework that clinical staff needed to understand what type of waste went where. It was the foundational text, and it served the NHS well. But the world moved on.

The 2013 Edition: Responding to Legislative Change

Seven years later, the 2013 revision arrived. The healthcare landscape had changed significantly. There were updates to environmental permitting regulations, the Carriage of Dangerous Goods regulations had been revised, and the NHS was under growing financial pressure to manage costs more effectively. The 2013 edition responded to these changes: it updated the regulatory framework, refined the colour-coding guidance, and added sector-specific guidance covering everything from ambulance services to GP surgeries and dental practices.

But the 2013 edition was still primarily a technical compliance guide. It told you what to do with waste, but it did not fundamentally challenge how healthcare organisations thought about waste. It remained in force for ten years — a long time in a rapidly evolving regulatory and environmental landscape.

The 2023 Edition: A Paradigm Shift

Published on 7 March 2023, the current edition of HTM 07-01 is not simply a revision. It is, in many respects, a reinvention. The document itself states clearly that it replaces HTM 07-01 (2006) and its subsequent 2013 revision. But what makes 2023 different is not just updated legislation — it is the shift in philosophy.

What changed in 2023?

The 2023 edition was published alongside the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy (also March 2023) and the two documents are designed to be read together. The strategy provides the "why" — the NHS's overarching ambition to transform waste management over ten years. HTM 07-01 provides the "how" — the technical and managerial framework to achieve it.

The 2023 edition restructured the document into a clearer two-part framework. Chapters 2–4 provide context and background: the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, principles of sustainable waste management, and the legislative framework. Chapters 5 and 6 provide the applied guidance: the technical approach to classification, segregation, colour-coding, and storage (Chapter 5), and the management approach — policy, training, contracts, audit, and emergency preparedness (Chapter 6). That Chapter 6 is entirely new and we will devote a full lesson to it later in this module.

👨‍🏫

In my experience working with NHS trusts, many organisations updated their procedures for the 2013 edition and then assumed the job was done for the next decade. The 2023 edition requires a more fundamental rethink — particularly around offensive waste and the 20:20:60 targets. You cannot treat this as a minor tweak. Teams that approach the 2023 guidance thinking "we just need to update our SOPs" invariably find themselves falling short of the targets within 12 months.

What Is HTM 07-01? Cutting Through the Jargon

Let's be very precise here, because this distinction matters enormously and is frequently misunderstood.

Health Technical Memorandum (HTM) A series of guidance documents produced by NHS England (and previously the Department of Health) covering health-specific aspects of building and engineering services. HTMs are best practice guidance, not legislation. They do not have the force of law. However, compliance with HTM 07-01 is the benchmark against which NHS organisations are expected to manage healthcare waste — and non-compliance can indicate breaches of underlying legislation.

This is a critical point. HTM 07-01 is not legislation. You cannot be prosecuted solely for failing to comply with HTM 07-01. However, the underlying law — the Environmental Protection Act 1990, the Hazardous Waste Regulations, the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations, the Carriage of Dangerous Goods regulations, and others — absolutely does carry legal force. HTM 07-01 tells you how to comply with all of that legislation in the specific context of healthcare waste. It is the how-to guide that sits on top of the law.

Legal Position: Important

While HTM 07-01 itself is guidance rather than law, failure to follow it can be used as evidence of poor practice in regulatory enforcement action. The Environment Agency, the Health and Safety Executive, and the Care Quality Commission all reference HTM 07-01 in their inspection frameworks. Practically speaking, non-compliance with HTM 07-01 carries significant regulatory, reputational and legal risk.

"Health Technical Memorandum (HTM) 07-01 'Safe and sustainable management of healthcare waste' provides a framework for best practice waste management across the UK to help healthcare organisations and other healthcare waste producers meet legislative, technical and policy requirements, as well as plan for future challenges and opportunities." — HTM 07-01, 2023 Edition, Paragraph 1.1

Notice the key phrase: framework for best practice. HTM 07-01 sets the standard. In the NHS context, that standard is effectively mandatory in practice, even if not in strict law.

Who Does HTM 07-01 Apply To?

The scope of HTM 07-01 is broader than many people assume. It is not simply a document for large NHS acute trusts — it applies to the full spectrum of healthcare waste producers in England.

The 2023 edition explicitly states that it has been developed based on the legislative and regulatory environment in England, and should not be relied upon by devolved nations. Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland have their own frameworks (Scotland uses the NHS Scotland colour-coding system, for example). If you work in a devolved nation, you should be aware of local variations.

Primary Sectors Covered

Sector Examples Typical Waste Types
NHS Trusts & Foundation Trusts Acute hospitals, mental health trusts, community trusts Full spectrum: clinical, offensive, domestic, pharmaceutical, anatomical
Primary Care GP surgeries, health centres, walk-in centres Sharps, clinical dressings, pharmaceutical, offensive
Ambulance Services NHS ambulance trusts, patient transport services Sharps, clinical waste, PPE, body fluid waste
Dental Practices NHS and private dental surgeries Amalgam, sharps, infectious waste, pharmaceutical
Laboratories NHS and private pathology labs Infectious substances, anatomical, chemical
Veterinary Practices Veterinary surgeries and hospitals Similar profile to human healthcare; guidance is applicable
Care Homes & Community Care Residential care, domiciliary care Offensive waste, sharps (insulin users), incontinence waste

One area that often surprises people is domiciliary care and self-care settings. Patients managing conditions at home — diabetics using insulin, patients on chemotherapy, those with stoma care needs — all generate healthcare waste. HTM 07-01 provides guidance on how this should be managed, even when generated in a domestic setting. Local authorities and clinical commissioning groups have responsibilities here.

Example: A GP Surgery's Scope of Responsibility

Consider a typical GP surgery. It generates sharps from immunisations and blood draws, orange and yellow bag clinical waste from wound dressings and infectious material, pharmaceutical waste (medicines returned by patients, expired stock), and offensive waste such as used incontinence pads from patients with mobility issues. HTM 07-01 applies to every single one of those waste streams. The GP practice manager needs to understand the colour-coding system, maintain appropriate storage facilities, ensure staff training is up to date, hold appropriate contracts with a licensed waste carrier, and produce duty of care documentation for every waste consignment that leaves the premises.

The Scale of the Challenge: NHS Waste in Numbers

To understand why HTM 07-01 matters at the strategic level, you need to appreciate the sheer scale of waste generated by the NHS. In 2016/17, NHS providers in England alone generated over 590,000 tonnes of waste — encompassing clinical waste, food waste, office materials, packaging, medical devices, and single-use items. That is not a small operational detail. That is an industry-scale environmental and financial challenge.

590,000Tonnes of waste generated by NHS providers in England annually
156,000Tonnes of clinical waste produced each year by the NHS

Clinical waste — which must be managed to the standards set out in HTM 07-01 — represents a significant proportion of this total. The cost of managing it is substantial, and the environmental impact, particularly from incineration, is considerable. This is the context in which you need to place HTM 07-01: it is not just a health and safety document, it is a strategic lever for the NHS to reduce its costs and its carbon footprint.

The Shift to Sustainability and the Circular Economy

This is perhaps the most significant conceptual change in the 2023 edition, and it is worth spending some time on it because it reshapes how you should think about your role as a healthcare waste professional.

In previous editions, the primary goal was safe disposal. The question was: "How do we get rid of this waste without harming people or the environment?" The 2023 edition reframes the question entirely: "How do we manage our resources — and our waste — in a way that achieves net zero carbon, eliminates avoidable waste, and treats waste as a resource wherever possible?"

The Waste Hierarchy

The 2023 edition places great emphasis on the waste hierarchy — a legally recognised framework that prioritises actions in this order:

  1. Prevention — Do not generate the waste in the first place. Could reusable equipment replace single-use items?
  2. Preparing for re-use — Can items be cleaned, sterilised and used again?
  3. Recycling — Can materials be recycled (packaging, paper, glass)?
  4. Other recovery — Energy from Waste (EfW) for residual waste
  5. Disposal — Landfill or incineration as a last resort

HTM 07-01 2023 explicitly states that circular economy approaches — such as replacing single-use products with reusable ones — reduce transport demand, reduce emissions, and improve cost efficiency. This is a fundamental shift from viewing waste management as an end-of-pipe problem to viewing it as a whole-lifecycle challenge.

NHS Net Zero: The Big Picture Driver

You cannot understand the 2023 edition without understanding its relationship to the NHS Net Zero programme. The NHS has committed to:

  • Net zero for direct emissions (Scope 1 and 2) by 2040
  • Net zero for indirect emissions (Scope 3 — supply chain, patient travel, etc.) by 2045
  • A 50% reduction in carbon emissions from waste management by 2026
  • An 80% reduction in carbon emissions from waste management by 2028

Incineration — the predominant treatment for clinical waste — is carbon-intensive. Every yellow bag of clinical waste that contains items that should have gone into an orange bag or a tiger stripe bag represents unnecessary carbon emissions. Every over-classified waste stream represents money spent on expensive incineration when cheaper, lower-carbon alternatives were available. Correct segregation, as mandated by HTM 07-01, is therefore not just a regulatory requirement — it is a core component of the NHS's net zero strategy.

Key Insight: The Sustainability Dimension

The 2023 edition introduces a genuinely new frame for thinking about waste management. You are no longer just a person who ensures waste goes in the right bag. You are a sustainability professional whose decisions about segregation directly affect the NHS's carbon footprint, financial sustainability, and legal compliance. This is a significant elevation of the waste management role — and it requires a commensurate elevation in knowledge and strategic thinking.

Risk-Based Thinking: A New Approach

One of the most practically important shifts in the 2023 edition is the move from a universal precautions model to a risk-based approach to waste classification.

In the universal precautions model (which informed much of the earlier guidance), the default was to treat all waste that might conceivably be infectious as if it were definitely infectious. Better safe than sorry. The result? Enormous quantities of waste were over-classified. Incontinence pads, sanitary products, non-medicated wound dressings, gloves used for non-clinical tasks — all ended up in yellow or orange clinical waste bags when most of them had no infectious risk at all.

The 2023 edition changes this. It asks: Does this waste actually present an infectious risk, based on its known properties? If a patient is not known to have an infectious condition, and the waste item — such as an incontinence pad — is not from a patient in isolation with a notifiable infection, then that waste is offensive waste, not clinical waste. It goes in the tiger stripe bag, not the yellow bag.

Important Clarification

The shift to a risk-based approach does not mean lowering your guard on genuine infectious waste. Waste from patients with notifiable infectious diseases, items contaminated with Category A pathogens (such as Ebola), sharps, anatomical waste, and pharmaceutical waste all remain subject to strict handling requirements. The risk-based approach means being accurate — neither over-classifying non-infectious waste as infectious, nor under-classifying genuinely infectious waste as offensive.

The Structure of HTM 07-01: Your Navigation Guide

Before you close this lesson, let me give you a clear map of the document so you know exactly where to find things when you need them in practice.

Chapter Title Key Content Who Needs It Most
Chapter 1 Introduction Purpose, scope, who should use the guidance, structure overview Everyone
Chapter 2 Strategic Objectives NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, 20:20:60 targets, Net Zero links Directors, Waste Managers
Chapter 3 Principles in Sustainable Waste Management Waste hierarchy, circular economy, carbon impact, social outcomes Waste Managers, Sustainability leads
Chapter 4 Legislative Framework England, Wales, Scotland, NI legislation; Duty of Care; EWC codes Waste Managers, Compliance teams
Chapter 5 Technical Approach Classification, segregation, colour-coding, storage, sharps, transport All clinical and waste-handling staff
Chapter 6 Management Approach Policy, training, contracts, waste manager role, audit, emergency preparedness Waste Managers, Facilities Managers

Spotlight: How a Community Trust Used HTM 07-01 to Reframe Waste

A community NHS trust in the South West, responsible for district nursing and community therapy services, had historically managed all clinical waste through a single yellow bag contract. When the 2023 edition was published, the Trust's Estates Manager reviewed its waste profile and realised that community nurses visiting patients in their own homes — administering wound care, delivering stoma support, providing catheter care — were placing virtually all their waste into yellow clinical bags by default, regardless of the patient's infection status.

A review of district nursing waste revealed that over 60% of the waste generated on community visits was offensive waste — incontinence pads, non-medicated dressings, hygiene items — being unnecessarily classified as clinical HTI waste. The trust introduced a simple two-bin system in community nursing bags (orange bag for clinical, tiger stripe for offensive) and provided a one-page laminated decision guide for nurses to carry in their visit bags. Within six months, orange and tiger stripe waste accounted for 65% of community nursing waste, and the annual waste disposal cost had fallen by £47,000.

Reflection: Taking Stock of What You Now Know

You have now covered a substantial amount of ground. Let's consolidate it with a reflection exercise before you move to Lesson 2.

☐Can you explain the difference between the 2006, 2013, and 2023 editions of HTM 07-01 in your own words?
☐Can you clearly articulate why HTM 07-01 is not legislation — and why that distinction matters in practice?
☐Can you name at least four different healthcare sectors to which HTM 07-01 applies?
☐Can you explain the connection between HTM 07-01, the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, and the NHS Net Zero programme?
☐Can you describe in simple terms what the shift from universal precautions to a risk-based approach means for waste classification?
👨‍🏫

If you are new to healthcare waste management, don't be overwhelmed by the scope of HTM 07-01. It is a comprehensive document, but it is well structured, and you do not need to memorise every paragraph. What matters is understanding the framework — knowing which chapter addresses which type of question — so that when you encounter a real-world issue, you know exactly where to look. In Lesson 2, we are going to get into the specific numbers: the 20:20:60 segregation targets and what they mean for your organisation's operations and finances.

lessons.title: Key Concepts: The 20:20:60 Segregation Targets
lessons.content:
The Numbers That Are Changing the NHS

In Lesson 1, you learned that the 2023 edition of HTM 07-01 represents a philosophical shift — from compliance-only to sustainability-driven waste management. Now we need to get specific. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, published alongside HTM 07-01 in March 2023, contains a set of precise, time-bound targets that every NHS organisation is expected to meet. Central to those targets is a number that has become shorthand for the entire transformation: 20:20:60.

In this lesson, we're going to take that number apart completely. We will look at what each component means, why the 60% offensive waste target is genuinely transformative, what the financial and carbon implications are, and — critically — why so many NHS organisations currently fall far short of these ratios and what that failure actually costs.

Learning Objectives
  • Understand the full NHS Clinical Waste Strategy and its six areas of focus
  • Precisely define HTI, AT, and OW and the treatment pathway for each
  • Understand why the 60% OW target is financially and environmentally transformative
  • Quantify the impact of waste over-classification — the core problem the strategy addresses
  • Know the complete timeline of strategy milestones from 2023 to 2030
  • Connect segregation targets to NHS Net Zero carbon reduction commitments
"By segregating waste in line with this strategy, NHS providers could reduce their HTI requirements by up to 35%, AT infrastructure requirements by up to 61% and increase OW demand by up to 229%." — NHS Clinical Waste Strategy Appendices, NHS England (March 2023)

The Over-Classification Problem in Context

To understand why the 20:20:60 target represents a transformation rather than an incremental improvement, consider that the NHS's current baseline — based on ERIC 2020/21 data — sits at roughly 65% HTI, 25% AT, and 10% OW for most large acute trusts. This means the NHS is sending three times as much waste to incineration as the target requires, and generating one-sixth of the offensive waste it should. The financial and carbon gap between current state and target state is not a rounding error — it represents a structural misallocation of resources that has persisted for decades, primarily driven by a culture of risk aversion in waste classification and a historical absence of offensive waste infrastructure.

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy: Six Strategic Priorities

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy is a rolling ten-year strategy with annual reviews. It was designed to "make a step change in NHS waste management practices" — not incremental improvement, but structural transformation. It is organised around three overarching themes — Net Zero Carbon, Resilience, and Productivity — and six strategic priority areas.

Priority Area Strategic Goal Key Measure of Success Target Date
Data Improve data quality and reporting accuracy 95% accuracy in clinical waste reporting 2024
Workforce Build waste management expertise across the NHS 100% of NHS trusts with dedicated waste manager (≥0.5 FTE) 2023
Compliance Ensure all staff understand and adhere to requirements Achieve 20:20:60 segregation targets across all NHS providers 2026
Commercial Develop commercial models supporting healthy market conditions Reduce average net cost of clinical waste management by 15% per tonne 2030
Infrastructure Invest in resilient infrastructure that meets future demand All NHS providers with offensive waste stream arrangements in place 2023
Sustainability Reduce harmful emissions and achieve net zero 50% carbon reduction from waste management (leading to 80% by 2028) 2026

Notice how these priorities interconnect. You cannot achieve the compliance target (20:20:60 by 2026) without the workforce target (waste managers in post by 2023). You cannot achieve the financial target (15% cost reduction by 2030) without achieving the compliance target. And none of it contributes to net zero without proper data to measure progress against. This strategy is designed as an integrated system — not a checklist of isolated initiatives.

Defining the Three Waste Streams: HTI, AT, and OW

The 20:20:60 ratio refers to three distinct clinical and healthcare waste treatment streams. You must understand exactly what each one means, because misclassification — putting waste in the wrong stream — is the root cause of the financial and environmental problems the strategy is designed to fix.

HTI: High Temperature Incineration (Target: 20%)

High Temperature Incineration (HTI) Thermal destruction of waste at temperatures typically above 850°C, with secondary combustion chambers reaching 1100°C or above. This treatment is reserved for the most hazardous healthcare waste — waste that cannot be safely treated by any other method. It is the most expensive treatment option and the highest in carbon emissions.

Waste classified for HTI includes:

  • Yellow stream clinical waste: Waste that is infectious (or potentially infectious) AND is contaminated with medicines or pharmaceutical products — for example, medicated wound dressings, iodine-soaked swabs, contrast media
  • Cytotoxic and cytostatic waste (purple stream): Any waste contaminated with chemotherapy agents — deeply hazardous to those who handle it and the environment
  • Anatomical waste (red stream): Identifiable human tissue, organs, body parts
  • Infectious waste from Category A pathogens: Waste from patients with conditions such as Ebola, Lassa fever, or variant CJD

The 20% HTI target tells you something important: only one-fifth of all NHS healthcare waste should require this intensive, expensive, carbon-heavy treatment. Right now, far more than 20% goes to HTI — because waste is being over-classified. We will come to the numbers shortly.

AT: Alternative Treatment (Target: 20%)

Alternative Treatment (AT) Treatment methods other than incineration that render infectious waste safe for onward disposal. The primary method is autoclaving (steam sterilisation), but other approaches include microwave treatment and chemical disinfection. AT-treated waste can typically be sent to energy-from-waste (EfW) facilities or, after appropriate treatment, to landfill.

Waste classified for AT includes:

  • Orange stream clinical waste: Infectious or potentially infectious waste that does NOT contain pharmaceutical products. Examples include wound dressings from infected wounds, PPE from infection risk areas, and waste from patients in infectious disease isolation
  • Orange-lidded sharps containers: Sharps not contaminated with pharmaceuticals — for example, those used for dry needling, blood glucose monitoring, or venepuncture in patients not on cytotoxic medications

AT is less expensive than HTI and has a lower carbon footprint. Orange bag waste costs significantly less per tonne to process than yellow bag waste. Moving waste from yellow to orange where clinically appropriate is one of the most straightforward cost-reduction levers available — and it is entirely achievable through better staff training and accurate classification.

OW: Offensive Waste (Target: 60%)

Offensive Waste (OW) Waste that is non-infectious and does not require specialist disposal, but which may cause offence due to its appearance, the presence of recognisable healthcare-related items, body fluids, or odour. Offensive waste can be managed in the same way as municipal waste — typically through energy-from-waste facilities. It is the lowest cost and lowest carbon treatment pathway for healthcare waste.

This is the stream where the transformation really happens. Offensive waste includes:

  • Incontinence pads and continence products from patients not known to be infectious
  • Sanitary products from staff and patients
  • Non-medicated wound dressings from non-infected wounds
  • Gloves and aprons used for non-clinical tasks (cleaning, food service)
  • Nappies
  • Stoma bags from patients not in infectious isolation
  • Nasal secretions on tissues
  • Urine and faeces from patients not known to be infectious (contained within appropriate receptacles)
The Key Test for Offensive Waste

Ask yourself: does this waste item come from a patient with a known or suspected infectious condition? Is the ward or area under infection control restrictions? Is the waste contaminated with pharmaceutical products? If the answer to all three is "no," the waste is almost certainly offensive waste — tiger stripe bag territory, not yellow or orange.

The Over-Classification Crisis: What the Data Reveals

Here is the uncomfortable truth. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy appendices — based on ERIC data from 2020/21 — reveal a stark picture of how far NHS organisations currently are from the 20:20:60 targets. And the financial and environmental implications are profound.

According to the strategy's analysis, based on waste management observations and audits:

35%Reduction in HTI requirements achievable through proper segregation
61%Reduction in AT infrastructure requirements achievable through proper segregation
229%Increase in offensive waste demand that proper segregation would create

Read those numbers again. A 35% reduction in incineration and a 61% reduction in alternative treatment. These are not marginal improvements — these are transformational changes achievable not through new technology or expensive infrastructure, but through correct waste classification by existing staff using existing containers.

The 229% increase in offensive waste demand tells you what is currently happening: huge quantities of offensive waste — incontinence pads, non-infected wound dressings, general hygiene waste — are being placed into clinical waste bags and processed through expensive HTI or AT routes when they should be going into tiger stripe bags and treated as offensive waste.

Why Is This Happening?

There are several interconnected causes of waste over-classification in the NHS:

  1. Risk aversion: Many clinical staff take the view that it is "safer" to put waste in a yellow bag than risk under-classifying infectious material. This instinct is understandable but misguided — it creates enormous unnecessary cost and carbon impact.
  2. Inadequate training: Classification decisions are often made by the person at the point of waste generation — a healthcare assistant, a cleaner, a nurse — who may not have received sufficient training to distinguish offensive from infectious waste.
  3. Infrastructure gaps: Until recently, many NHS organisations did not even have tiger stripe bag infrastructure in place. If there is no offensive waste container available, waste defaults to clinical bags.
  4. Habit and culture: "We've always done it this way" is a powerful force in large organisations. Yellow bags have been the default clinical waste container for decades, and changing that default requires sustained change management effort.
  5. Absence of dedicated waste management expertise: Without a qualified waste manager in post, there is no one to audit, challenge, and improve segregation practices.
👨‍🏫

In audits I have conducted at NHS trusts, it is common to find that 40–50% of the contents of yellow clinical waste bags have no infectious risk whatsoever. Incontinence pads, non-infected dressings, tissues — all being incinerated at significant cost and carbon impact. When you show clinical staff the data — the cost per tonne, the carbon equivalent, the percentage of their ward's waste that should be in tiger bags — the reaction is usually a mixture of surprise and genuine motivation to change. The data is a powerful change management tool.

The Financial Case: Why 60% Offensive Waste Saves Money

The financial argument for achieving the 20:20:60 targets is compelling. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy identifies potential savings of approximately £11 million per year for the NHS as a whole through correct waste segregation.

Understanding why requires understanding the cost differential between treatment streams:

Treatment Stream Relative Cost per Tonne Carbon Intensity Typical Destination
HTI (Yellow) Highest (index: 100) Highest Licensed incinerator
AT (Orange) Medium (approx. 60–70) Medium Autoclave → EfW or landfill
OW (Tiger) Lowest (approx. 20–30) Lowest Energy from Waste (EfW) facility
Domestic (Black) Near zero or credit Minimal EfW, recycling, landfill

When you transfer a tonne of waste from the HTI stream to the OW stream — because it was over-classified as infectious when it was actually offensive — you are potentially reducing your cost by 70–80% per tonne on that waste. At the scale of the NHS, with tens of thousands of tonnes of clinical waste generated each year, those savings rapidly become very large numbers indeed.

The 15% reduction in average net cost per tonne targeted by 2030 is not going to come from renegotiating contracts alone. It is going to come primarily from moving waste down the treatment hierarchy through accurate segregation. Every trust that achieves the 60% offensive waste target is contributing to that national commercial objective.

Key Financial Principle

Correct waste segregation is one of the highest-return, lowest-capital-cost interventions available to an NHS estate and facilities team. The investment required is training, containers, signage, and management oversight — all significantly less expensive than the ongoing savings from reduced incineration costs.

The Carbon Case: Connecting Segregation to Net Zero

The carbon argument for the 20:20:60 targets is just as compelling as the financial argument. Incineration is a carbon-intensive process. Energy-from-waste is lower in emissions. Autoclaving is lower still. Every tonne of waste correctly identified as offensive and diverted from incineration to EfW represents a measurable reduction in the NHS's Scope 1 and 2 carbon footprint.

The sustainability milestone in the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy is a 50% reduction in carbon emissions from waste management by 2026, rising to 80% by 2028. This is an aggressive target, and achieving it depends very substantially on achieving the 20:20:60 segregation ratio. The two targets are inseparable.

Beyond incineration, HTM 07-01 2023 introduces sustainability thinking at every level of the waste hierarchy. Reducing the use of single-use plastic products — PPE, packaging, single-use medical devices — reduces the volume of waste generated in the first place. Choosing reusable over single-use where clinically appropriate moves waste prevention up the hierarchy. The circular economy framework embedded in the 2023 edition asks NHS organisations to engage with their supply chains to design out waste at the product level, not just manage it better at the end-of-pipe level.

The Complete Timeline: Milestones You Need to Know

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy sets out a clear chronological sequence of targets. As a healthcare waste professional, you need to know these dates and what they require:

2023 — Two Critical Milestones

Workforce: 100% of NHS trusts and foundation trusts to have a dedicated waste manager role (at least 0.5 FTE), with accountable individuals for waste management identified across all providers.

Infrastructure: All NHS providers to introduce arrangements for the management of the offensive waste stream — bins, signage, labelling, and collection arrangements in place.

2024 — Data Milestone

Data: All clinical waste generated by NHS providers is regularly reported with a minimum of 95% accuracy. This requires robust waste tracking systems, accurate completion of duty of care documentation, and regular data verification against actual waste movements.

2026 — The Big Target

Compliance: All NHS providers achieve clinical waste segregation targets of 20:20:60 HTI, AT, and OW.

Sustainability: 50% reduction in carbon emissions from waste management in NHS organisations.

2028 — Carbon Target

Sustainability: 80% reduction in carbon emissions from waste management.

2030 — Commercial Target

Commercial: The average net cost of clinical waste management reduces by 15% per tonne of waste.

Is Your Organisation on Track? Assessing Current Position

The most important question for you as a practitioner is: where does your organisation currently sit against these targets? The honest answer for most NHS providers, based on the ERIC data, is: far behind.

Here is a simple framework for assessing your current position:

☐Waste composition audit: Has your organisation conducted a recent waste composition audit to determine what percentage of your clinical waste is genuinely infectious vs. offensive?
☐Tiger bag infrastructure: Are tiger stripe offensive waste bins available in all areas where offensive waste is generated — including wards, outpatient departments, staff areas, and patient accommodation?
☐Current stream data: Do you have accurate data on what percentage of your waste currently goes to HTI, AT, and OW treatment? Is it captured at 95% accuracy?
☐Waste manager in post: Is there a dedicated waste manager (at least 0.5 FTE) with clear accountability for waste management across your organisation?
☐Training in place: Have all staff with waste generation responsibilities received training on the difference between infectious, offensive, and domestic waste?
☐Gap analysis: Based on your current stream data vs. the 20:20:60 target, what is your gap — and what would closing that gap save per year in disposal costs?

Practical Exercise: Calculating the Potential Impact

Exercise: The Cost of Over-Classification

Imagine your organisation generates 500 tonnes of clinical/offensive waste per year. Currently, your split is 60% HTI, 25% AT, and 15% OW — a common profile for an NHS trust that has not yet fully implemented offensive waste infrastructure.

Using the strategy's evidence that up to 60% of current HTI/AT waste could be reclassified as OW with proper segregation, calculate:

  1. What would your waste volumes look like under the 20:20:60 target? (100 tonnes HTI, 100 tonnes AT, 300 tonnes OW)
  2. If HTI costs £400/tonne and OW costs £80/tonne, what is the current annual cost vs. the target cost?
  3. What is the potential annual saving from achieving the 20:20:60 target?

Current cost calculation: (300 × £400) + (125 × £240) + (75 × £80) = £120,000 + £30,000 + £6,000 = £156,000/year. Target cost: (100 × £400) + (100 × £240) + (300 × £80) = £40,000 + £24,000 + £24,000 = £88,000/year. Potential saving: ~£68,000/year — achievable through training and containers alone. Scale this to a large trust generating 3,000 tonnes/year, and you are looking at potential savings of over £400,000 annually.

👨‍🏫

The financial calculation above is a powerful tool in making the internal business case for waste management improvement. When you present the 20:20:60 targets to a Finance Director or a Chief Operating Officer, lead with the numbers. The cost of getting this right is modest — additional tiger bag infrastructure, training time, signage. The cost of leaving the status quo in place is measurable, recurring, and growing every year as waste volumes increase. The business case writes itself.

lessons.title: Regulations: The Colour-Coded Waste Segregation System
lessons.content:
The Language Every Healthcare Waste Professional Must Speak

The colour-coded waste segregation system is the practical backbone of HTM 07-01. Every other element of the guidance — the 20:20:60 targets, the management framework, the contract requirements — depends on this system being implemented correctly, every day, by every member of staff who generates or handles waste. Get the segregation right and everything else follows. Get it wrong, and no amount of good strategy or well-written policy will prevent the over-classification problem that costs the NHS hundreds of millions of pounds every year.

In this lesson, we are going to work through every colour-coded waste stream in detail. We will cover what goes in, what does not go in, which EWC codes apply, how containers must be sealed, and the most common segregation errors you will encounter in practice. By the end of this lesson, you should be able to walk onto any NHS ward, look at a clinical room waste station, and immediately identify what is right and what needs correcting.

Learning Objectives
  • Identify and describe each colour-coded waste stream mandated by HTM 07-01 2023
  • Know exactly which waste items belong to each stream
  • Understand the EWC codes that apply to each stream
  • Recognise the most common segregation errors
  • Understand the requirements for sharps containers, including the swan-neck seal method
  • Apply the risk-based classification test to common clinical scenarios
7Colour-coded waste streams mandated by HTM 07-01 2023
18 01 03*Primary EWC code for infectious clinical waste requiring special disposal
"This colour-coded system must be utilised to aid the consistent identification and segregation of waste." — HTM 07-01 2023 Edition, Chapter 5, Paragraph 5.64

The Cost of a Single Misclassified Bag

Consider this scenario, which illustrates the compound effect of individual segregation errors. A ward generates 15 yellow clinical waste bags per day. An audit reveals that 8 of those 15 bags contain predominantly offensive waste — incontinence pads and non-medicated dressings from patients with no infectious diagnosis. Each yellow bag weighs approximately 3 kg. That is 24 kg of offensive waste per day being sent to incineration at approximately £420/tonne, when it should be in tiger stripe bags heading to an Energy from Waste facility at approximately £65/tonne. The cost difference on that single ward: £2.67 per day, £973 per year, on just one ward. Multiply across 40 wards and you have £38,920 per year in avoidable over-treatment costs — from just this one over-classification pattern on this one waste item category.

The Framework: Why Colour Coding Matters

HTM 07-01 states that the colour-coded system must be utilised to aid consistent identification and segregation of waste. The word "must" here is significant — this is not optional guidance but a mandatory requirement for NHS England organisations. The system creates a visual language that reduces the need for individual judgement at the point of waste generation: if you know the colour, you know the treatment pathway.

Devolved Nation Variation

NHS Scotland uses a different colour-coding system from NHS England. The HTM 07-01 2023 system described in this lesson applies to England. If you work with organisations in Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland, always check local guidance. This is particularly important when dealing with waste that crosses national boundaries — for example, when a Scottish NHS ambulance transfers a patient to an English hospital.

The colour-coding system serves four purposes simultaneously:

  1. Safety: It signals the hazard level of the contents to everyone in the handling chain — ward staff, porters, drivers, treatment plant operatives
  2. Compliance: It maps directly onto the regulatory treatment requirements — certain EWC codes mandate specific treatment methods
  3. Efficiency: It enables waste to be directed to the most cost-effective and environmentally appropriate treatment from the point of generation
  4. Auditability: It creates a visible, physical record of waste classification decisions that can be audited

Stream 1: Yellow — Infectious Waste for High Temperature Incineration

Yellow Stream (Infectious/HTI) Waste that is infectious or potentially infectious AND is contaminated with pharmaceutical/medicinal products, or waste from patients with highly infectious conditions. Must be sent for high temperature incineration (HTI). This is the most expensive and most carbon-intensive treatment pathway.

What goes in the yellow bag/container:

  • Medicated wound dressings (e.g., dressings impregnated with iodine, silver, or other pharmaceuticals) from infected wounds
  • Swabs, wipes, and gauze contaminated with pharmaceutical products in an infectious context
  • Waste from patients with Category A or highly infectious diseases (e.g., variant CJD, Ebola, Lassa fever)
  • Contrast media-contaminated waste
  • Waste from patients under strict infectious isolation where pharmaceutical-contaminated items are present
  • Materials contaminated with prion-associated waste

What does NOT go in the yellow bag:

  • Non-medicated wound dressings from non-infected wounds (these are offensive waste — tiger bag)
  • PPE used for non-infectious patients (offensive waste)
  • Incontinence pads from non-infectious patients (offensive waste)
  • Sharps — these have their own sharps containers (see below)
  • Cytotoxic/cytostatic waste (purple stream)

EWC Codes (Yellow — Infectious):

  • 18 01 03* — Wastes whose collection and disposal is subject to special requirements in order to prevent infection (hazardous — marked with asterisk)
The Yellow Bag Overuse Problem

Yellow bags are the most widely misused container in NHS waste management. They are frequently used as a "catch-all" default when staff are uncertain. Any waste from a clinical setting should not automatically go in a yellow bag. The question is: is this waste actually infectious AND pharmaceutical-contaminated, or does it merely look like it might be clinical? Most of the time, in most clinical areas, the answer is that a significant proportion of apparent yellow bag waste is actually orange bag or tiger bag waste. This is the heart of the over-classification problem.

Stream 2: Orange — Infectious Waste for Alternative Treatment

Orange Stream (Infectious/AT) Waste that is infectious or potentially infectious but is NOT contaminated with pharmaceutical products, and does not require HTI. Can be treated by alternative treatment methods such as autoclaving. Less expensive and lower carbon than yellow stream.

What goes in the orange bag:

  • Wound dressings from infected wounds where no pharmaceutical products are involved
  • PPE (gloves, aprons, masks) used when treating patients with infectious conditions
  • Waste from infectious patient areas (e.g., side rooms for patients with C. diff, MRSA, norovirus)
  • Stoma bags from patients with known infectious gastroenteric conditions
  • Body fluid-contaminated waste where there is a known or suspected infectious risk
  • Microbiology laboratory waste that has been deactivated or is for AT treatment

EWC Codes (Orange — Infectious):

  • 18 01 03* — As with yellow, infectious waste subject to special requirements (hazardous)
  • Note: The distinction between yellow and orange is in the treatment method contracted, not the EWC code. Your waste management contract must specify whether a consignment goes to HTI or AT.
Practical Scenario: Orange vs. Yellow

A ward nurse is changing the dressing on a wound infected with MRSA. The dressing is a simple saline-moistened gauze pad — no pharmaceutical impregnation. What colour bag does it go in? Orange — infectious waste, but not pharmaceutical-contaminated. Now the same nurse changes a dressing on a different patient where an iodine-impregnated dressing was used. The patient has a wound infection. What colour bag? Yellow — infectious waste WITH pharmaceutical contamination (the iodine). The same basic clinical activity produces different waste stream outputs depending on the products used.

Stream 3: Purple — Cytotoxic and Cytostatic Waste

Purple Stream (Cytotoxic/Cytostatic) Waste contaminated with cytotoxic or cytostatic medicinal products — chemotherapy agents and other medicines that are toxic to cells. These are deeply hazardous materials that require special handling and must be sent for high temperature incineration at a licensed facility authorised to receive such waste. This is also sometimes designated as yellow bags with purple stripes.

What goes in the purple stream:

  • Used chemotherapy bags, lines, syringes, and administration sets
  • PPE worn by pharmacy staff and nurses when preparing or administering cytotoxic drugs
  • Vomit or body fluid waste from patients within 7 days of receiving cytotoxic treatment (the window during which the drugs remain active in body fluids)
  • Sharps contaminated with cytotoxic/cytostatic agents (in purple-lidded sharps containers)
  • Unused or partially used cytotoxic medicines

EWC Codes (Cytotoxic/Cytostatic):

  • 18 01 08* — Cytotoxic and cytostatic medicines (hazardous)
  • 18 01 10* — Amalgam waste from dental care (separate category)
Critical Safety Warning: Cytotoxic Waste

Cytotoxic waste must never be placed in any stream other than purple. These drugs are carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic — they cause cancer, genetic damage, and foetal harm. Exposure to cytotoxic waste through incorrect segregation poses a genuine occupational health risk to cleaners, waste handlers, and autoclave plant workers who would be exposed if the waste reached a non-HTI treatment facility. This is not a cost efficiency issue — it is a patient and staff safety imperative.

Stream 4: Tiger Stripe (Yellow/Black) — Offensive Waste

Tiger Stripe / Offensive Waste Stream Non-infectious, non-hazardous waste that may cause offence due to its appearance or the presence of body fluids, but which does not present an infectious risk. Can be managed via energy-from-waste facilities. Significantly cheaper and lower carbon than clinical waste streams. The 2023 edition's key target is to get 60% of NHS waste into this stream.

What goes in the tiger stripe bag:

  • Incontinence pads and nappies from patients not known to be infectious
  • Sanitary products (menstrual waste) from patients and staff
  • Non-medicated wound dressings from non-infected wounds (clean surgical wounds, simple abrasions)
  • Gloves and aprons from non-clinical tasks (cleaning, food service, administrative handling)
  • Stoma bags from patients not under infectious precautions
  • Urine bags from catheterised patients not under infectious precautions
  • Nasal secretions on tissues
  • Saliva-contaminated items from patients not under infectious precautions
  • PPE from non-infected patient encounters (routine examination, non-infectious clinical areas)

What does NOT go in the tiger stripe bag:

  • Sharps of any kind
  • Waste from patients in infectious isolation
  • Any pharmaceutical-contaminated waste
  • Cytotoxic waste
  • Anatomical waste
  • Blood bags or significant liquid blood waste

EWC Codes (Offensive Waste):

  • 18 01 04 — Wastes whose collection and disposal is NOT subject to special requirements in order to prevent infection (non-hazardous — no asterisk)
Infrastructure Requirement (2023 Target)

One of the 2023 milestones in the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy was for all NHS providers to introduce arrangements for the management of the offensive waste stream. This means tiger stripe bags must be available in every area where offensive waste is generated — which includes most clinical areas, all patient accommodation areas, staff changing and hygiene facilities, and outpatient areas. If tiger bags are not physically present and accessible, staff will default to clinical waste bags, perpetuating over-classification.

Stream 5: Black — Domestic Waste

Black Stream (Domestic/Municipal Waste) General non-clinical, non-infectious, non-hazardous waste — the same type of waste generated in any office, home, or retail environment. Can be managed through standard municipal waste routes: energy-from-waste, recycling, landfill as appropriate.

What goes in the black bag:

  • Office waste: paper, cardboard (where not recyclable), food packaging
  • Food waste from non-clinical kitchens (subject to separate food waste requirements)
  • General household/residential-type waste from patient accommodation (non-clinical items)
  • Recyclable materials should be separated into recycling streams before the remainder goes to black bags

The contamination problem: Black bags in clinical settings frequently become contaminated with clinical items — sharps, blood-contaminated materials, pharmaceutical waste — because staff use them as a convenient general container. A single contaminated black bag can compromise an entire collection load, requiring the whole load to be treated as clinical waste at much higher cost. Regular auditing of black bag contents is an important part of any waste management programme.

Stream 6: Blue — Pharmaceutical Waste

Blue Stream (Pharmaceutical/Medicinal Waste) Pharmaceutical and medicinal waste that does not meet the definition of cytotoxic/cytostatic. Includes returned, expired, or unwanted medicines that require appropriate disposal. Must be sent to a licensed pharmaceutical waste contractor for incineration or recovery.

What goes in the blue-lidded container or blue bag:

  • Expired or unwanted medicines returned from wards or by patients
  • Partially used medications that cannot be returned to pharmacy
  • Creams, ointments, lotions that require disposal
  • Liquid medicines
  • Sharps contaminated with non-cytotoxic pharmaceutical products (blue-lidded sharps containers)

EWC Codes (Pharmaceutical):

  • 18 01 09 — Medicines other than those in 18 01 08 (i.e., non-cytotoxic pharmaceuticals — non-hazardous)
