  • 20 01 32 — Medicines other than those mentioned in 20 01 31 (household/community route)

Sharps Containers: A System Within the System

Sharps management is one of the highest-risk areas of healthcare waste handling, and HTM 07-01 provides very specific guidance on the containers required for different types of sharps waste. The lid colour of a sharps container tells you exactly what it contains and where it must go.

Lid Colour Contents Treatment Typical Use
Yellow lid Sharps contaminated with pharmaceutical products HTI (incineration) Ward drug rounds; insulin syringes where patient receives medications
Orange lid Sharps for alternative treatment — NOT contaminated with pharmaceuticals AT (autoclave) Blood glucose monitoring lancets; dry needling sharps; venepuncture with no pharmaceutical contamination
Purple lid Sharps contaminated with cytotoxic/cytostatic agents HTI (specialist licence) Oncology/chemotherapy areas; any sharp used with cytotoxic drugs
Blue lid Sharps contaminated with non-cytotoxic pharmaceutical products (alternative categorisation) HTI or specialist Varies by contractor specification — always check your contract
Critical: Never Mix Sharps Streams

A single cytotoxic sharp (purple lid) in a yellow-lidded or orange-lidded container immediately contaminates the entire container and everything in it, requiring disposal as cytotoxic waste. This has significant cost implications and, more importantly, presents a serious safety risk to autoclave and treatment facility workers. Sharps container lid colours must be strictly observed.

The Swan-Neck Seal: Closing Sharps Containers Correctly

One of the most commonly incorrectly performed procedures in clinical waste management is the closure of sharps containers. HTM 07-01 specifies that sharps containers must be sealed using the swan-neck seal method when the container is full (to no more than the fill line — typically 2/3 full).

The swan-neck seal works as follows:

  1. Ensure the container is not filled above the fill line indicator
  2. Close the temporary aperture (the slot or hole through which sharps are deposited)
  3. Rotate the lid to the locked position — this creates the swan-neck seal by folding the opening mechanism over
  4. Check the container is fully sealed and cannot be re-opened
  5. Label with the point of generation, date of closure, and responsible person details
  6. Place in appropriate UN-approved outer packaging for transport
👨‍🏫

In clinical areas, the two most common sharps container errors I encounter are overfilling (sharps containers filled past the fill line, creating needle stick risk when the lid is being locked) and using the wrong lid colour. A yellow-lidded sharps container in a phlebotomy department that does no drug administration is costing money that could be saved by switching to orange lids — the sharps have no pharmaceutical contamination. This sounds like a small detail but across a large trust with hundreds of sharps containers in use at any time, the cost differential is meaningful.

Additional Specialist Streams

Red: Anatomical Waste

Red Stream (Anatomical Waste) Recognisable human tissue, organs, body parts, and placentae. Must be sent for HTI (incineration) at a licensed facility authorised to receive anatomical waste. Subject to additional legal requirements regarding dignity and consent in some circumstances.

EWC Code: 18 01 02 — Body parts and organs including blood bags and blood preserves (hazardous).

Anatomical waste requires separate storage from other waste streams, a dedicated collection arrangement, and specific consignment notes. Some anatomical waste (particularly stillborn remains and foetal tissue after a certain gestation) may also be subject to requirements under the Human Tissue Act 2004 and relevant NHS guidance on the sensitive disposal of such remains. This is an area where waste management intersects with patient dignity and requires careful handling.

Amalgam and Dental Waste

Dental practices generate a specific waste type — amalgam — that requires specialist disposal. Amalgam is a mercury-containing material and is subject to strict environmental controls. HTM 07-01 provides guidance on amalgam separators, recycling contractors, and the prohibition on amalgam disposal to the sewer system or as regular waste.

EWC Code: 18 01 10* — Amalgam waste from dental care (hazardous).

Common Segregation Errors: Your Top 10 to Watch For

Based on NHS waste audits and compliance inspections, these are the most common segregation errors found in healthcare settings:

  1. Non-infected incontinence pads in yellow bags — Should be tiger bag
  2. Non-medicated dressings from clean wounds in orange bags — Should be tiger bag if non-infectious
  3. Food packaging and cardboard in black bags rather than recycling — Missed recycling opportunity
  4. Gloves from non-clinical tasks in orange bags — Should be tiger bag if used for cleaning, portering, food service
  5. Sharps containers overfilled past the fill line — Presents needlestick risk during closure
  6. Yellow-lidded sharps containers used in areas with no pharmaceutical administration — Should be orange lid if no pharmaceutical contamination
  7. Medicines (tablets, blister packs) in black domestic waste bags — Should be blue pharmaceutical stream
  8. Entire waste bag discarded in wrong container at collection point — Ward-level segregation lost at final collection
  9. Liquid pharmaceutical waste poured down drains — Environmental offence; should be collected in liquid pharmaceutical waste containers
  10. Cytotoxic waste placed in yellow rather than purple stream — Safety and regulatory failure
Scenario-Based Test: Where Does It Go?

Test yourself on these common scenarios. Cover the answers and work through each one:

Scenario A: A used insulin syringe from a diabetic patient on a ward where the patient receives no other injectable medications. Answer: Yellow-lidded sharps container (pharmaceutical contamination — insulin is a pharmaceutical product).

Scenario B: A used blood glucose lancet from a diabetic patient on the same ward. Answer: Orange-lidded sharps container (no pharmaceutical contamination — lancet touched only blood, no drug).

Scenario C: An incontinence pad from a patient with norovirus in a side room under contact precautions. Answer: Orange bag (infectious, but no pharmaceutical contamination).

Scenario D: An incontinence pad from a patient on a general medical ward with no infectious diagnosis, no isolation precautions. Answer: Tiger stripe bag (offensive waste — no infectious risk).

Scenario E: A partially used bag of IV cyclophosphamide (chemotherapy). Answer: Purple stream (cytotoxic/cytostatic).

Container Labelling and Documentation

Correct segregation is only the first step. Every waste container must be appropriately labelled before it leaves the point of generation, and the overall waste movement must be documented through a duty of care transfer note (or, for hazardous waste, a consignment note).

HTM 07-01 requires that containers are labelled with:

  • The name and address of the organisation producing the waste
  • The date the container was closed
  • The waste type (using the colour coding and EWC code as appropriate)
  • A unique identifier where required for tracking purposes
☐Are all waste bags and containers labelled with the point of generation and date?
☐Are hazardous waste consignment notes completed for all hazardous waste movements?
☐Are duty of care transfer notes retained for all waste consignments?
☐Is the UN-approved packaging used for sharps containers when transported?
☐Are waste storage areas locked, signed, and separate from other operational areas?
👨‍🏫

One thing I always emphasise in training: the colour-coded system only works if it is consistent from the point of generation all the way to the treatment facility. A beautifully segregated ward can have all its effort undone at the waste collection point if bags from different streams are consolidated into the wrong container, or if an unlabelled bag ends up in an incorrect stream through poor storage area management. The system is only as strong as its weakest point — and the weakest point is often the transition between clinical area and the central waste storage facility.

lessons.title: Practical Application: Chapter 6 — Management Approach
lessons.content:
6Sections covered in HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 Management Approach
0.5 FTEMinimum dedicated waste manager requirement by 2023 per NHS Clinical Waste Strategy
"Introducing a waste segregation target of 20:20:60 for all NHS providers and trusts. Include compliance requirements within the responsibilities, accountabilities, training, and induction programmes across the NHS workforce." — NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, NHS England (March 2023) — Strategic Priority: Compliance

Before and After: The Impact of Structured Management

An NHS mental health trust with six inpatient sites and a large community estate had never appointed a dedicated waste manager. Waste management was distributed across six site facilities managers, none of whom had formal waste management training. When the trust underwent a CQC inspection that included an estates and environment review, the inspector found: no current duty of care transfer notes for three of the six sites, two expired waste carrier licences for contractors, no evidence of staff waste training since 2019, and a waste management policy referencing HTM 07-01 2013 with no mention of offensive waste. The trust received a Requirement Notice. Within 12 months of appointing a 0.6 FTE Waste Manager and implementing the Chapter 6 framework, all compliance gaps had been closed and the trust had achieved a 28% reduction in clinical waste costs through better segregation and contract renegotiation.

From Guidance to Governance: How HTM 07-01 Gets Implemented

Everything we have covered in the first three lessons — the history and intent of HTM 07-01, the 20:20:60 targets, the colour-coded segregation system — represents the what of healthcare waste management. Chapter 6 of HTM 07-01 2023 answers the question of how: how does an NHS organisation actually implement and sustain all of this in practice? This chapter, which is entirely new to the 2023 edition, is arguably the most operationally important section of the whole document for those in management or leadership roles.

Chapter 6 is titled "Management Approach" and it covers six interconnected areas: policy, education and training, contract management, the waste manager role, continuous improvement and audit, and emergency preparedness. In this lesson, we will work through each of these in depth, with practical templates, checklists, and the kind of specific guidance that you can take directly into your organisation.

Learning Objectives
  • Understand what an HTM 07-01-compliant waste management policy must contain
  • Know the training requirements for different staff groups
  • Understand the elements of effective contract management for clinical waste
  • Define the waste manager role as specified by HTM 07-01 and the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy
  • Design a continuous improvement and audit programme for waste management
  • Understand the requirements for emergency preparedness in waste management

Section 1: Policy Requirements

A healthcare organisation's waste management policy is the foundational document that gives practical legal and operational authority to everything described in HTM 07-01. Without a robust, current, well-disseminated policy, all the technical guidance in the world remains aspirational rather than operational.

HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 specifies what a compliant waste management policy must address. It is not sufficient to have a document titled "Waste Management Policy" that was last reviewed in 2017 and simply references the old 2013 guidance. The 2023 edition requires a policy that reflects current standards, current targets, and current organisational realities.

What Your Policy Must Cover

Policy Element What It Must Address Why It Matters
Scope and Purpose Who the policy applies to; all waste types covered; relationship to legislation and HTM 07-01 2023 Sets the legal and operational framework; ensures no waste type or staff group is excluded
Roles and Responsibilities Named accountable individuals at Board, Director, Waste Manager, and operational levels Creates clear accountability — essential for compliance enforcement and incident management
Segregation Requirements Colour-coded system, which streams are used by the organisation, point-of-generation requirements The operational instruction that staff need to make correct daily decisions
Storage Requirements How waste is stored on-site, security, signage, maximum storage durations, environmental controls Regulatory requirement under the Environmental Permitting Regulations; safety and security
Transport and Documentation Duty of care requirements, consignment note procedures, carrier licensing checks Legal requirement — failure to issue duty of care transfer notes is a criminal offence
Training Requirements Who requires training, frequency, record-keeping, induction requirements Ensures competence across the workforce; required for CQC and regulatory compliance
Incident Reporting What constitutes a waste management incident, how to report, investigation procedures Enables learning from failures; regulatory requirement for RIDDOR-reportable incidents
Audit and Review Frequency and methodology of waste audits, who conducts them, action planning from findings Drives continuous improvement; provides evidence of compliance for regulators
Emergency Procedures Actions in case of waste spillage, container failure, fire in waste storage areas, contractor failure Regulatory requirement; operational resilience during incidents
Net Zero and Sustainability How the policy supports NHS Net Zero commitments; connection to the 20:20:60 targets New requirement in 2023 edition — links waste management to organisational sustainability strategy
Policy Currency

A waste management policy that still references HTM 07-01 2013 is not compliant with current best practice. If your organisation's policy pre-dates March 2023 and has not been updated to reference the 2023 edition and the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy targets, it needs to be revised urgently. Regulatory inspections by the CQC, Environment Agency, or NHS England will identify out-of-date policies as a non-compliance finding.

Section 2: Education and Training

Training is where policy meets practice. You can have the most comprehensive waste management policy in the NHS, but if the healthcare assistant who is changing an incontinence pad at 3am does not know the difference between offensive and infectious waste, the over-classification problem will continue regardless.

HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 specifies that all staff who generate, handle, or manage healthcare waste must receive appropriate training. "Appropriate" means matched to their role and responsibilities — not one-size-fits-all, but targeted and proportionate.

Training Requirements by Staff Group

Staff Group Core Training Content Frequency
All staff (induction) Basic waste segregation; colour-coding system; what goes where on their work area; reporting procedure Induction; annual refresher minimum
Clinical staff (nurses, HCAs, doctors) All above PLUS: infectious vs. offensive classification; risk-based approach; sharps safety and container types; pharmaceutical and cytotoxic waste Induction; annual refresher; update when guidance changes
Waste handlers / porters All above PLUS: safe handling of waste bags; UN packaging requirements; storage area procedures; what to do if a bag is torn or leaking; incident reporting Induction; annual refresher; practical competency assessment
Estates and facilities managers HTM 07-01 in full; contract management; duty of care documentation; 20:20:60 targets; audit methodology; emergency procedures Role commencement; biennial minimum; update with every new guidance edition
Waste managers All above PLUS: full HTM 07-01 2023; NHS Clinical Waste Strategy; environmental law; procurement and contract management; audit design; carbon and sustainability metrics Comprehensive initial training; annual CPD; professional qualification pathway recommended
Board and executive level Strategic overview: NHS Clinical Waste Strategy; 20:20:60 targets; financial and carbon impact; regulatory risk; organisational accountability Briefing at appointment; annual strategic update

Training must be documented. HTM 07-01 requires that records of training completion are maintained and available for inspection. A training record that simply shows an e-learning module was completed is insufficient — there must be evidence of competency, not just completion. For high-risk activities (sharps handling, cytotoxic waste management), practical competency assessments are appropriate.

Training Delivery Methods

HTM 07-01 does not prescribe specific training delivery methods — it requires effective outcomes. Organisations use a range of approaches: e-learning for basic awareness (accessible, scalable, recordable), face-to-face workshops for deeper content, ward-level practical sessions for clinical staff (using real containers and scenarios), and tool-box talks for specific updates. The most effective waste management training programmes combine all of these, with e-learning for knowledge and face-to-face or practical sessions for application.

Section 3: Contract Management

For most NHS organisations, healthcare waste disposal is managed through contracts with specialist waste management companies. The clinical waste contract is typically one of the most significant supply contracts held by an NHS trust's estates and facilities directorate — both in terms of value and in terms of risk.

Chapter 6 of HTM 07-01 provides specific guidance on contract management, recognising that poor contract management is one of the key factors that perpetuates over-classification and prevents the NHS from achieving its 20:20:60 targets.

What Good Contract Management Looks Like

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy calls for the use of standardised waste service contracts and best-practice contract management. The key elements of an HTM 07-01-compliant contract management approach are:

1. Specification and Scope

Your contract must specify every waste stream you generate, the treatment method required for each stream, collection frequencies, and container specifications. A contract that simply says "clinical waste disposal" without specifying stream-by-stream requirements is inadequate. You need separate line items for HTI, AT, OW, pharmaceutical, cytotoxic, and anatomical waste as appropriate to your organisation's profile.

2. Carrier and Facility Licensing Verification

Your contractor must hold a current Waste Carrier Registration with the Environment Agency (or equivalent devolved authority). Any treatment facility used by the contractor must hold the appropriate Environmental Permit for the waste types being treated. You are legally required, under your Duty of Care, to verify these licences — not just trust the contractor's word that they are in place. Licence details should be verified at contract start and annually thereafter, or whenever a contractor changes treatment facility.

3. Waste Tracking and Data

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy's 2024 target for 95% data accuracy means your contract must include robust waste tracking provisions. You should receive regular reports from your contractor showing exactly how much waste (by stream, by EWC code) was collected from your organisation, when, by whom, and where it was taken for treatment. These data are essential for monitoring progress toward the 20:20:60 target and for the accurate ERIC reporting that NHS trusts are required to submit.

4. Performance Monitoring

Your contract should include KPIs for contractor performance: collection frequency adherence, response times for incidents, data reporting timeliness, complaints management, and sustainability metrics. Regular review meetings (at least quarterly for significant contracts) should review performance against these KPIs and address any shortfalls.

5. Non-Compliance Provisions

What happens when the contractor fails? Missed collections, vehicle breakdowns, and treatment facility incidents do occur. Your contract must specify what the contractor's obligations are in these circumstances, including the maximum permissible storage period for uncollected waste and the arrangements for emergency collection if normal service is disrupted. This connects directly to emergency preparedness, which we will cover below.

Contract Management Scenario

An NHS trust's waste management data shows that 70% of its waste is going to HTI treatment — far above the 20:20:60 target. The waste manager investigates and discovers that the clinical waste contractor is consolidating orange bag waste and tiger bag waste into the same vehicle as yellow bag waste, and billing all of it at the HTI rate. The trust is being overcharged, and its data is showing a worse segregation profile than the actual ward-level practice. By reviewing its contractor's weigh bridge records and vehicle tracking data, the trust identifies the problem and issues a formal KPI non-compliance notice — recovering overcharges and requiring the contractor to separate vehicle loads for each stream. This is why contract monitoring matters.

Section 4: The Waste Manager Role

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy's 2023 workforce target — 100% of NHS trusts to have a dedicated waste manager of at least 0.5 FTE — signals recognition at the highest level of the NHS that waste management cannot be run as a side-task added to someone's existing role. Effective waste management requires dedicated expertise, dedicated time, and dedicated accountability.

What the Waste Manager Role Requires

Dedicated Waste Manager (HTM 07-01 Chapter 6) A named individual with clear accountability for the organisation's waste management performance, holding at least 0.5 FTE dedicated to this function. The role encompasses policy management, staff training, contractor oversight, data management, audit, and strategic improvement toward the 20:20:60 targets and NHS Net Zero commitments.

The waste manager's key responsibilities under HTM 07-01 include:

  • Policy ownership: Maintaining the waste management policy to reflect current guidance, legislation, and organisational practice
  • Training coordination: Ensuring all relevant staff groups receive appropriate, current training; maintaining training records
  • Contract management: Acting as the day-to-day contract manager for waste disposal contracts; verifying carrier licences; monitoring contractor performance
  • Data management: Collecting, verifying, and reporting waste data accurately; producing ERIC returns; tracking progress against 20:20:60 targets
  • Audit programme: Designing and running waste composition audits; reviewing segregation compliance; identifying and acting on non-compliance
  • Incident management: Responding to waste management incidents; conducting root cause analysis; implementing corrective actions
  • Emergency preparedness: Maintaining emergency procedures; testing contingency arrangements; liaising with contractors on resilience planning
  • Sustainability reporting: Measuring and reporting carbon impacts from waste management; contributing to organisational net zero plans
  • Innovation: Identifying and implementing opportunities to reduce waste, improve segregation, and adopt circular economy approaches
The 0.5 FTE Minimum

The requirement for at least 0.5 FTE is a minimum, not a recommendation. For large acute trusts generating thousands of tonnes of waste per year, with dozens of waste streams, complex oncology and laboratory waste profiles, and multiple sites, 0.5 FTE is entirely insufficient. The 0.5 FTE minimum is the floor for the smallest NHS providers. The waste manager role in a major teaching hospital or large mental health trust with community sites should be a full-time or multiple-FTE function. Organisations that have appointed a waste manager at 0.1 FTE or added it as an afterthought to an existing job description have not met the spirit or intent of the requirement.

👨‍🏫

If you are the person in your organisation who has just had "waste manager" added to your existing role without any reduction in your other responsibilities, I want to acknowledge that this is a real and frustrating problem across the NHS. The 0.5 FTE requirement was included in the strategy precisely because the evidence shows that organisations without dedicated waste management capacity consistently perform worse on segregation, data accuracy, and compliance. If you find yourself in this position, my advice is to use the financial and carbon data from Lesson 2 to make the business case for properly resourced waste management at your organisation — and to use this course as part of building the expertise that underpins that case.

Section 5: Continuous Improvement and Audit

Continuous improvement is not a slogan in HTM 07-01 — it is a structured requirement. Chapter 6 specifies that NHS organisations must operate an ongoing programme of waste management audit and improvement, not just respond reactively to incidents or regulatory findings.

Types of Waste Management Audit

An effective audit programme for healthcare waste management should include three types of audit, each serving a different purpose:

1. Compliance Audit

This checks whether the organisation is meeting its legal and policy obligations. It covers:

  • Are duty of care transfer notes being completed correctly and retained?
  • Are consignment notes for hazardous waste accurate and archived for three years minimum?
  • Are carrier licences for all contractors current and verified?
  • Are waste storage areas meeting Environmental Permitting requirements?
  • Are staff training records current?

2. Segregation Audit (Waste Composition Audit)

This is the most operationally valuable type of audit — and the most revealing. A waste composition audit involves physically opening and auditing the contents of clinical waste bags from specific areas to determine what is actually in them vs. what should be in them. Done systematically, it reveals the real-world gap between policy and practice.

A basic waste composition audit protocol:

  1. Select a representative sample of bags from each clinical area (at least 20% of bags from each area in a given week)
  2. Using appropriate PPE, open bags in a controlled environment (waste storage area or waste management facility)
  3. Categorise each item: correctly classified, should be orange (misclassified as yellow), should be tiger (over-classified as clinical), should be domestic (in wrong container)
  4. Record the proportion of correctly vs. incorrectly classified items by area
  5. Calculate the financial cost of misclassification (tonnes × cost differential between treatment streams)
  6. Report findings by area with specific examples for targeted training
  7. Set improvement targets and a re-audit schedule
Audit Frequency Recommendation

HTM 07-01 does not mandate a specific audit frequency — it requires a programme of continuous improvement. For organisations just beginning their 20:20:60 improvement journey, quarterly waste composition audits are appropriate. Once segregation reaches near-target levels, twice-yearly may be sufficient. The important thing is that findings lead to action, and action leads to re-audit to verify improvement.

3. Sustainability Audit

A relatively new addition to the audit toolkit, driven by the 2023 edition's sustainability emphasis. This covers:

  • Carbon emissions from waste management (by stream, calculated against ERIC data)
  • Waste reduction initiatives in progress and their measured impact
  • Single-use plastic reduction progress
  • Progress toward circular economy objectives (reusable equipment, sustainable procurement)
  • Progress toward 20:20:60 segregation targets expressed as carbon reduction

Section 6: Emergency Preparedness

What happens when the normal waste management system fails? This is a question that the COVID-19 pandemic — which created sudden, massive surges in clinical waste volumes — brought into sharp relief for every NHS waste manager in the country. HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 requires that all NHS organisations have current, tested emergency preparedness arrangements for waste management.

Scenarios That Require Emergency Procedures

  • Contractor failure: Primary waste contractor goes into administration, loses its licence, or is unable to collect due to vehicle or facility problems
  • Demand surge: Sudden increase in waste volumes (pandemic, major incident, outbreak) exceeding normal collection capacity
  • Storage facility incident: Fire, chemical spill, or structural failure in waste storage areas
  • Transport incident: Vehicle accident during waste transport; spillage of clinical waste on public roads
  • Treatment facility failure: Incinerator or autoclave breakdown resulting in backlog of waste awaiting treatment
  • Infectious waste exposure incident: Torn bag, needle stick injury, or exposure of staff or public to waste contents

Emergency Plan Requirements

☐Identified alternative waste contractors with pre-agreed contingency contracts for emergency collection
☐Maximum permissible storage capacities documented for each waste type and storage area
☐Named incident commander for waste management emergencies, with deputisation arrangements
☐Communication cascade: who to notify (Environment Agency, NHS England, Infection Control, site managers) and in what order
☐Spillage response procedure: appropriate PPE, decontamination materials, reporting requirements
☐Annual test/exercise of emergency procedures — not just a plan on paper but an exercised response
☐Integration with the organisation's broader Business Continuity Plan and Major Incident Plan
Regulatory Requirement: Duty of Care in Emergencies

Your Duty of Care under the Environmental Protection Act 1990 does not pause during a waste management emergency. If your normal contractor cannot collect, you remain legally responsible for ensuring your waste is stored safely and is eventually collected by a licensed carrier and taken to an authorised facility. Using an unlicensed emergency carrier to solve a collection backlog problem will not be accepted as a mitigating factor if the waste is subsequently mishandled. Contingency contracts with secondary licensed carriers are not a luxury — they are a legal necessity.

Bringing It Together: A Management Framework Template

Here is a practical one-page framework you can use to assess or communicate your organisation's Chapter 6 compliance status:

☐Policy: Waste management policy updated to reference HTM 07-01 2023; all required elements present; approved at Board level; communicated to all staff
☐Training: Role-specific training delivered and recorded for all staff groups; induction process includes waste management; annual refresher scheduled
☐Contracts: All contracts specify treatment streams and methods; carrier licences verified; KPIs in place; quarterly performance reviews scheduled
☐Waste Manager: Dedicated post in place at ≥0.5 FTE; clear job description; named accountable individual at Board level; CPD programme in place
☐Audit: Waste composition audit programme active; findings reported and actioned; progress against 20:20:60 tracked quarterly; sustainability metrics recorded
☐Emergency Preparedness: Emergency plan current and approved; contingency contractor arrangements in place; plan tested in last 12 months; integrated with organisational BCP
👨‍🏫

Chapter 6 is, in my view, the most practically impactful chapter in the 2023 edition — and it is the one that was entirely absent from the 2013 version. If you have been working in healthcare waste management for a long time, you will recognise most of these elements as good practice that you have been doing informally. What Chapter 6 does is formalise that good practice, give it an authoritative basis, and connect it to the organisation's strategic and legal obligations. Use Chapter 6 to build a governance framework, not just a list of tasks.

lessons.title: Case Study: Implementing HTM 07-01 in an NHS Trust
lessons.content:
Learning from a Real-World Transformation

Throughout this module, you have built a comprehensive understanding of HTM 07-01 2023 — its history, its targets, its colour-coded segregation system, and the management framework in Chapter 6. Now we are going to bring all of that together in a single, detailed case study that follows one fictional NHS organisation — Greenfield NHS Foundation Trust — through an 18-month transformation from poor compliance to near-achievement of the 20:20:60 targets.

Greenfield NHS Foundation Trust is a composite based on patterns observed across multiple NHS transformations. Every data point, challenge, and solution described here reflects real scenarios encountered during genuine NHS waste management improvement programmes. Follow along, reflect on what you would do differently at each stage, and think about how the lessons apply to your own organisational context.

Learning Objectives
  • Apply the full framework of HTM 07-01 2023 to a realistic NHS implementation scenario
  • Understand the change management approach required for successful waste management transformation
  • Analyse how waste audits reveal the true scale of over-classification
  • Understand how training translates into measurable operational improvements
  • Calculate the financial and carbon return on a structured HTM 07-01 implementation
  • Recognise the barriers to implementation and how they are overcome
Change ManagementThe structured approach to transitioning individuals, teams, and organisations from a current state to a desired future state. In the context of HTM 07-01 implementation, effective change management means combining technical solutions (containers, signage, contracts) with people-focused interventions (training, leadership engagement, peer champions) to achieve sustained behavioural change in waste segregation practice.

Greenfield NHS Foundation Trust: The Starting Point

Organisation Profile (Month 0)

Greenfield NHS Foundation Trust is a medium-large acute NHS trust serving a mixed urban and rural population. It operates two hospital sites: a 650-bed main acute hospital (Greenfield General Hospital) and a 200-bed community hospital (Meadowview Hospital) with outpatient, diagnostics, and step-down care functions.

The trust also has contractual responsibility for clinical waste management across twelve community health centres and GP-aligned clinical facilities.

Annual clinical waste volume (Month 0): Approximately 950 tonnes of clinical and offensive waste per year, plus 1,800 tonnes of domestic and other waste.

Waste management spend (Month 0): £2.3 million per year on clinical waste disposal.

Current segregation profile (Month 0):

  • HTI (Yellow stream): 75% of clinical waste
  • AT (Orange stream): 20% of clinical waste
  • OW (Tiger stripe): 5% of clinical waste

This represents a serious deviation from the 20:20:60 target — the trust is sending more than three times as much waste to incineration as it should, and barely one-twelfth of the intended offensive waste volume to the OW stream.

75%Greenfield's waste going to HTI at the start — vs. 20% target
£2.3MAnnual clinical waste spend before transformation began
"The improper management of offensive waste represents a significant cost to the NHS, and helping to address this issue should lead to financial savings as well as reduction in environmental harm." — HTM 07-01 2023 Edition, Paragraph 2.7

Phase 1: The Trigger and the Waste Audit (Months 1–3)

The Trigger: Publication of the 2023 HTM

The transformation at Greenfield was triggered by the publication of HTM 07-01 2023 and the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy in March 2023. The trust's Director of Estates and Facilities, Sarah Chen, read both documents carefully and immediately identified two problems: the trust had no dedicated waste manager (the role was distributed across four members of the estates team, none of whom had more than 0.1 FTE assigned to waste management), and the tiger stripe offensive waste stream had not been introduced on the main hospital site.

Sarah presented the risk to the trust's Board: the trust was potentially in breach of the 2023 workforce and infrastructure milestones in the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy, and was massively over-spending on incineration. The Board approved a twelve-month improvement programme with a dedicated budget of £85,000 for staff time, training, infrastructure, and audit costs.

Appointment of a Waste Manager

The first decision was to recruit a dedicated Waste Manager at 1.0 FTE — above the 0.5 FTE minimum, but appropriate for an organisation of Greenfield's size and complexity. James Okafor was appointed, having previously worked in environmental compliance for a waste management contractor and had a qualification in Environmental Management. His appointment began in Month 2.

Why Full FTE Matters Here

The trust's CFO initially challenged the cost of a full-time waste manager appointment, arguing that 0.5 FTE was sufficient to meet the minimum requirement. James's business case — which projected a £380,000 annual saving from achieving the 20:20:60 targets (see below) — demonstrated that a full-time appointment at a salary of approximately £35,000 would deliver a return on investment in under two months once the improvement programme achieved results. The CFO approved the appointment.

The Waste Composition Audit: Opening the Yellow Bags

Before any training or infrastructure change, James insisted on conducting a thorough waste composition audit. His rationale was straightforward: "We can't fix what we don't measure, and we can't convince clinical staff to change their behaviour without showing them real data from their own areas."

Over three weeks in Months 2–3, James and two estates colleagues conducted a systematic audit of yellow bag waste from every clinical area in Greenfield General Hospital and Meadowview Hospital. Using full PPE, they opened, weighed, and categorised the contents of approximately 340 yellow clinical waste bags.

Audit Findings (Month 3)

The findings were stark. Of the contents of 340 yellow bags audited:

  • 28% were correctly classified as requiring HTI (medicated dressings from infected wounds, waste from patients with confirmed infectious diagnoses, pharmaceutical-contaminated items in an infectious context)
  • 27% should have been in orange bags (infectious waste with no pharmaceutical contamination)
  • 45% should have been in tiger stripe bags (offensive waste — incontinence pads from non-infectious patients, non-medicated dressings from clean wounds, PPE from non-infectious patient encounters, sanitary waste)
  • 0% should have been in black bags (surprisingly, domestic waste contamination of yellow bags was minimal — suggesting that staff were at least making a distinction between clinical and domestic)

In other words, 72% of Greenfield's yellow bag waste did not need to be there. Only 28% genuinely required incineration.

The financial implication was immediately apparent. At the time of the audit, Greenfield was paying approximately £420 per tonne for HTI treatment. Orange stream AT was costing approximately £200 per tonne. Tiger stripe OW was costing approximately £65 per tonne.

Using ERIC data showing approximately 712 tonnes per year going to HTI treatment:

  • 475 tonnes (67% of yellow bag waste, correctly reclassified) could be moved to OW at £65/tonne — saving £355/tonne × 475 = £168,625/year on that portion alone
  • 192 tonnes could be moved to AT at £200/tonne — saving £220/tonne × 192 = £42,240/year
  • Total projected saving from yellow bag reclassification: approximately £210,000/year
👨‍🏫

James presented these findings to the Medical Director, Chief Nursing Officer, and Director of Finance in a single board-level briefing. He showed them photographs of the audit contents — area-by-area breakdowns, with real examples of incontinence pads, non-medicated gauze dressings, and boxes of nitrile gloves in yellow bags. The photographs were far more persuasive than any table of numbers. Senior clinical leaders who had previously treated waste management as an operational issue suddenly understood it as a governance, financial, and sustainability failure. This is a critical lesson in change management: data tells you what is wrong; images of the actual waste tell people why it matters.

Phase 2: Infrastructure and Training (Months 4–9)

Tiger Stripe Infrastructure Roll-Out

One of the immediate barriers identified in the audit was the absence of tiger stripe offensive waste infrastructure across the trust. On the main hospital site, tiger bags were available only in four areas — two postnatal wards and two care of the elderly wards. On the community hospital site, there were none at all.

James developed a phased roll-out plan for tiger stripe infrastructure, prioritising areas with the highest volume of offensive waste misclassified as yellow. In Month 4, tiger stripe bags, pedal-bin liners in appropriate sizes, and wall-mounted dispensers were procured through the NHS Supply Chain framework and installed in:

  • All inpatient wards (40 wards across both hospital sites)
  • All outpatient departments
  • All A&E and urgent care areas
  • Staff changing and welfare areas (sanitary waste)
  • All twelve community health centres

Total infrastructure cost: £18,400 (bins, dispensers, initial stock of bags). This was a one-off capital cost against a projected annual saving running into hundreds of thousands of pounds.

The Training Programme

James designed a three-tier training programme, recognising that different staff groups needed different depth of knowledge:

Tier 1: All-Staff Awareness (delivered Month 5–6)

A 20-minute e-learning module covering: what goes in which bag, the key distinction between offensive and infectious waste, and the three questions to ask before choosing a waste container. Completion was mandated as an addition to the trust's annual statutory and mandatory training requirements. 94% completion was achieved within eight weeks through ward-level management action.

Tier 2: Clinical Staff Deep Dive (delivered Month 6–8)

A 90-minute face-to-face session for all nursing staff, healthcare assistants, and AHP staff. Delivered ward-by-ward by James and two trained Infection Prevention and Control nurses (who had been separately briefed and trained as co-facilitators). The session used real waste bags, photographs from the audit, and scenario-based exercises. Ward-specific data from the audit was shared so that staff could see exactly how their own ward had performed — this ward-level specificity drove engagement that would not have been possible with generic messaging.

Tier 3: Waste Handler and Porter Training (delivered Month 5)

A half-day practical session covering safe handling of all waste streams, the importance of maintaining stream integrity from ward to central storage, recognising mislabelled containers, and incident reporting. Practical competency was assessed for all 23 waste-handling staff.

94%Staff e-learning completion within 8 weeks of launch
100%Waste handling staff completed practical competency assessment

Signage and Visual Management

James worked with a graphic designer to produce colour-coded, laminated waste segregation posters for every clinical area, sluice room, and waste collection point in the trust. The posters used photographs of actual waste items (from the audit) to illustrate what went where, rather than text descriptions. Research in behavioural science consistently shows that visual decision aids at the point of choice are more effective than written policies — the "point-of-use" principle.

Phase 3: Monitoring, Re-audit, and Fine-Tuning (Months 10–15)

Three-Month Re-Audit (Month 10)

Three months after the training programme was complete, James conducted a re-audit using the same methodology as the baseline audit. The results showed significant improvement:

Waste Classification Month 3 Audit (Baseline) Month 10 Re-Audit Change
Correctly in yellow (HTI) 28% 64% +36 percentage points
Should be orange (misclassified) 27% 20% -7 percentage points
Should be tiger (over-classified) 45% 16% -29 percentage points

The proportion of yellow bag waste that was correctly classified had more than doubled. The over-classification of offensive waste as yellow had fallen from 45% to 16%. This represented a significant improvement — though not yet at target levels. The remaining 16% incorrectly classified in yellow bags was the focus of Phase 3 work.

60%Reduction in segregation errors achieved after training and infrastructure programme

Targeted Interventions for Persistent Problem Areas

The re-audit revealed that the improvement was not uniform across all areas. Three wards — the Medical Admissions Unit, an Older Adults ward, and the A&E department — were still showing high rates of over-classification. In these areas, James arranged additional ward-level sessions, this time led by the ward sister or charge nurse (peer-led training having greater impact in clinical settings than training from an external facilitator), and installed additional signage at bin locations using simplified, single-question decision prompts: "Is this patient on infectious precautions? If no, use the TIGER BAG."

Contract Review and Re-Specification

With improved segregation data now available, James renegotiated the trust's clinical waste contract with its primary contractor. The new contract specified separate collection vehicles for HTI, AT, and OW streams — eliminating the risk of stream consolidation during collection. It also included quarterly waste composition reporting from the contractor's facilities, providing independent verification of the trust's internal data.

The new contract pricing, reflecting the changed stream mix, delivered an immediate reduction in per-tonne costs: the trust was now generating significantly less HTI waste and much more OW waste, with the contract pricing reflecting the lower-cost treatment methods.

Phase 4: The 18-Month Results

Greenfield NHS Foundation Trust — 18-Month Transformation Results

Segregation Profile (Month 18)

  • HTI (Yellow stream): 24% (was 75%; target 20%)
  • AT (Orange stream): 22% (was 20%; target 20%)
  • OW (Tiger stripe): 54% (was 5%; target 60%)

The trust is at 85% progress toward the 20:20:60 target — having moved from 5% offensive waste to 54% in 18 months. The remaining gap (6 percentage points on OW, 4 percentage points on HTI) is achievable in the following 12 months with continued training reinforcement and the targeted interventions underway in the three remaining high-error areas.

Financial Results (Month 18 annualised)

  • HTI disposal costs reduced by approximately £380,000 per year (through stream migration from yellow to orange and tiger)
  • OW disposal costs increased by approximately £46,000 per year (more volume at lower per-tonne rate)
  • Net annual saving: approximately £334,000 per year
  • Implementation costs (waste manager recruitment, training, infrastructure, audit): approximately £140,000 (over 18 months)
  • Payback period: approximately 5 months from annualised savings

Carbon Results (Month 18)

  • Incineration volume reduced by approximately 485 tonnes/year
  • Estimated carbon reduction equivalent: approximately 200 tonnes CO₂e per year (based on DEFRA carbon conversion factors for clinical waste incineration vs. EfW)
  • This represents approximately 38% progress toward the trust's waste management carbon reduction target under the NHS Net Zero programme
£334KNet annual saving achieved by Month 18
85%Progress toward 20:20:60 target in 18 months
5 monthsPayback period on full implementation investment

Lessons Learned: The Greenfield Change Management Framework

The Greenfield transformation was not primarily a technical achievement. The technical elements — the colour-coded containers, the segregation system, the audit methodology — were already well established in HTM 07-01. What Greenfield achieved was a change management success, and the lessons from that success are transferable to any NHS organisation.

Lesson 1: Data First, Action Second

James's instinct to conduct a thorough baseline audit before taking any action was the most important decision of the programme. The audit data transformed the conversation from abstract compliance requirement to concrete operational reality. It showed exactly where the problems were, exactly how large the financial cost was, and exactly which clinical areas needed the most attention. Without that data, the training programme would have been generic; with it, it was targeted and persuasive.

Lesson 2: Senior Leadership Engagement is Non-Negotiable

The programme succeeded because the trust's Director of Estates and Facilities had the credibility and access to take the findings to the Board, and because the Board gave it the governance and financial backing it needed. Waste management improvement programmes that are treated as purely operational initiatives without senior leadership backing consistently stall — usually at the point where clinical staff are asked to change their behaviour, because ward-level resistance is difficult to overcome without a clear organisational mandate.

Lesson 3: Peer-Led Training Outperforms Central Delivery

The most effective training in the Greenfield programme was delivered not by the waste manager but by ward sisters and charge nurses who had been trained and empowered as local waste champions. Their credibility with ward staff, their knowledge of the specific waste types generated in their area, and their ongoing daily presence made them far more effective than periodic visits from an estates professional.

Lesson 4: Infrastructure Must Precede Behavioural Change

Tiger stripe bags were rolled out before the training programme, not after. This sequencing was deliberate. It ensured that when staff were trained and motivated to change their behaviour, the physical means of doing so was already in place. Training staff to use tiger bags before the bags are available results in frustrated staff who lose motivation to change when the expected infrastructure doesn't materialise.

Lesson 5: Celebrate and Communicate Progress

At six months and twelve months, James produced simple one-page "waste performance dashboards" for each ward, showing their improvement against the baseline audit data. Ward sisters displayed these in staff areas. The combination of peer competition (which ward had improved most?) and genuine pride in tangible results drove continued engagement long after the initial training push had concluded.

Scenario: Applying the Greenfield Lessons to Your Organisation

Consider your own organisation (or the type of healthcare setting most relevant to you). Based on what you have learned in this case study:

  1. What do you think your current HTI/AT/OW split is? If you do not know, that is itself important information — it means no one is measuring it.
  2. What would be your first three actions if you were James Okafor at Greenfield? (Hint: measure, then engage leadership, then build the infrastructure.)
  3. Who would be your key allies in driving a training programme — is there a clinical champion who would be credible in your setting?
  4. What is the biggest barrier to change in your context — is it culture, infrastructure, training, data, leadership support, or something else?

Module 2 Summary: What You Have Learned

You have now completed all five lessons of Module 2: Demystifying HTM 07-01. Let's consolidate everything you have covered:

☐Lesson 1: HTM 07-01 2023 replaces both the 2006 and 2013 editions. It is best practice guidance, not legislation, but carries significant practical force. It applies to the full spectrum of healthcare waste producers. Its 2023 edition integrates sustainability, circular economy, and NHS Net Zero objectives for the first time.
☐Lesson 2: The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy's 20:20:60 targets require 20% HTI, 20% AT, and 60% OW by 2026. Achieving these targets requires reducing HTI by 35%, AT by 61%, and increasing OW by 229% — all achievable through better segregation alone. Financial savings of ~£11M across the NHS are projected.
☐Lesson 3: The colour-coded system covers seven streams: yellow (HTI), orange (AT), purple (cytotoxic), tiger stripe (OW), black (domestic), blue (pharmaceutical), and red (anatomical). Each stream has specific EWC codes and treatment requirements. Sharps containers have four lid colours corresponding to different contamination profiles. The swan-neck seal is required for all sharps container closure.
☐Lesson 4: Chapter 6 of HTM 07-01 2023 (new to this edition) requires all NHS organisations to have: a compliant waste management policy, role-specific training, well-managed contracts with licensed carriers, a dedicated waste manager (≥0.5 FTE), an active continuous improvement and audit programme, and tested emergency preparedness arrangements.
☐Lesson 5: The Greenfield case study demonstrates that achieving 85% progress toward the 20:20:60 target in 18 months is realistic, with a net financial saving of £334K/year, a 5-month payback period, and measurable carbon reductions. The key success factors are: data-led approach, senior leadership engagement, targeted training, infrastructure before behaviour change, and ongoing monitoring.
👨‍🏫

You have now covered the foundational knowledge that every healthcare waste professional needs to work effectively with HTM 07-01 2023. The document is not mysterious — it is detailed, well-structured, and genuinely designed to be used as a working reference. The challenge is not understanding it; the challenge is implementing it in complex organisations with competing priorities, limited resources, and ingrained habits. The Greenfield case study shows that it can be done, it can be done relatively quickly, and the financial and environmental returns are substantial. The rest of this course will build on this foundation, taking you into the more specialist areas — specific sector guidance, laboratory waste, pharmaceutical and cytotoxic waste management, and the legal framework in detail. Well done for completing Module 2.

quiz.q: What year was the current edition of HTM 07-01 published?
quiz.explanation: The current edition of HTM 07-01 was published in 2023, replacing the 2013 version.

quiz.q: What does the '20:20:60' target refer to in the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy?
options: 20% yellow bag, 20% orange bag, 60% tiger bag
quiz.explanation: The 20:20:60 target means 20% to high-temperature incineration, 20% to alternative treatment, and 60% classified as offensive waste.

quiz.q: What colour bag/container is used for offensive (non-infectious) healthcare waste under HTM 07-01?
quiz.explanation: Tiger bags (yellow with black stripes) are used for offensive/hygiene waste that is non-infectious but may cause offence to sight or smell.

quiz.q: According to HTM 07-01, what is the minimum staffing requirement for dedicated waste management in NHS Trusts?
options: At least 0.5 FTE dedicated waste manager
quiz.explanation: HTM 07-01 and the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy require 100% of NHS trusts to have a dedicated waste manager role of at least 0.5 FTE.

quiz.q: Which colour lid or container is used for cytotoxic and cytostatic medicinal waste?
quiz.explanation: Purple-lidded containers are specifically designated for cytotoxic and cytostatic medicinal waste under the HTM 07-01 colour-coded system.

quiz.q: HTM 07-01 is best described as:
options: Primary UK legislation on waste management
An EU regulation directly applicable in the UK
Best practice guidance (not legislation) published by NHS England
quiz.explanation: HTM 07-01 is best practice guidance published by NHS England. It is not legislation itself, but compliance with it is used by regulators as the benchmark for acceptable practice.

quiz.q: What is the key operational chapter of HTM 07-01 that covers the management approach to waste?
quiz.explanation: Chapter 6 of HTM 07-01 covers the Management Approach, including policy requirements, training, contract management, and audit requirements.

quiz.q: Under HTM 07-01, which of the following is a key driver for reclassifying infectious waste as offensive waste where appropriate?
quiz.explanation: The push to correctly classify waste as offensive rather than infectious reduces treatment costs and carbon emissions, supporting the NHS Net Zero programme.

quiz.q: What percentage accuracy target is set for clinical waste reporting by NHS organisations?
quiz.explanation: The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy target is for a minimum of 95% accuracy in clinical waste reporting by all NHS organisations.

quiz.q: The HTM 07-01 colour-coded system designates orange bags for:
options: Infectious waste suitable for alternative treatment
quiz.explanation: Orange bags/containers are used for infectious waste suitable for alternative treatment such as autoclaving, microwave, or chemical treatment.

modules.title: The Waste Hierarchy in Healthcare Practice
modules.description: Learn to apply the waste hierarchy (prevent, reuse, recycle, recover, dispose) specifically within healthcare settings, balancing infection control requirements with sustainability targets.
objectives: Apply the five levels of the waste hierarchy to healthcare waste scenarios
Identify opportunities for waste prevention and reduction in clinical settings
Evaluate recycling and reuse options for different healthcare waste streams
Design a waste minimisation plan for a healthcare facility
Calculate the cost and carbon impact of improved waste segregation

lessons.title: Overview: The Waste Hierarchy and Healthcare
lessons.content:
Welcome to Module 3

In this module, we move from understanding what healthcare waste is to understanding how we should deal with it — and in what order. That order is determined by a powerful piece of environmental policy called the waste hierarchy. By the end of this lesson, you will understand where the waste hierarchy comes from, what it requires, and why applying it in a healthcare context is more nuanced — and more important — than in any other sector.

The Legal Foundation: Where Does the Waste Hierarchy Come From?

The waste hierarchy is not a voluntary best practice — it is a legal duty. In England and Wales, it is enshrined in the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/988), which transpose the requirements of Directive 2008/98/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council — commonly called the Waste Framework Directive (WFD) — into domestic law.

Waste Framework Directive (Directive 2008/98/EC) The overarching EU environmental law that establishes the conceptual and legal framework for waste management across member states. Although the UK has left the EU, the domestic regulations transposing this Directive remain in force and continue to apply.

The specific provision you need to know is Article 4 of the Waste Framework Directive, which establishes the hierarchy as a priority order in waste prevention and management legislation and policy. Regulation 12 of the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011 implements this directly, requiring waste holders to apply the following order of priority:

  1. Prevention — avoiding waste arising in the first place
  2. Preparing for reuse — checking, cleaning or repairing products so they can be reused
  3. Recycling — reprocessing waste materials into the same or different products
  4. Other recovery — including energy recovery (incineration with energy recovery, anaerobic digestion)
  5. Disposal — landfill or incineration without energy recovery, as the last resort
"Member States shall take measures to encourage the options that deliver the best overall environmental outcome. This may require specific waste streams departing from the hierarchy where this is justified by life-cycle thinking on the overall impacts of the generation and management of such waste." — Article 4(2), Waste Framework Directive 2008/98/EC

That phrase — "life-cycle thinking" — is one you will return to repeatedly in this module. It acknowledges that the hierarchy is not absolute; there are circumstances where following it rigidly would produce worse environmental outcomes than departing from it. In healthcare, this tension is constant.

Post-Brexit note: The Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011 remain fully in force. Although the UK is no longer bound by EU law directly, the retained domestic legislation continues to apply the same hierarchy obligations. The Environment Act 2021 further embeds waste prevention principles into UK policy through producer responsibility and extended producer responsibility schemes.

The Five Tiers Explained

Tier 1: Prevention

Prevention sits at the very top of the hierarchy — it is the most desirable option. This means not generating waste at all: buying less, buying better, designing products with longer lifespans, substituting materials. In healthcare, this might mean switching from individually-wrapped single-use items to bulk dispensing, rationalising pharmaceutical stock to reduce expiry waste, or right-sizing procedure packs.

Tier 2: Preparing for Reuse

This tier covers products that, with some intervention — cleaning, inspection, minor repair — can be used again for the same purpose. In healthcare, the most significant example is reusable sharps containers. Rather than disposing of a plastic container after one use, it is collected, emptied under controlled conditions, sanitised, inspected, and returned to service. This directly addresses the enormous volume of single-use plastic generated by clinical settings.

Tier 3: Recycling

Recycling converts waste materials into new products. In healthcare, this includes PVC recycling (the RecoMed scheme for IV bags and oxygen masks), cardboard and paper recycling, segregated plastics, and food waste composting. Recycling in healthcare requires careful management: materials must be confirmed as non-hazardous before entering recycling streams, which creates both logistical and training demands.

Tier 4: Other Recovery

Recovery encompasses processes that extract value from waste that cannot be prevented, reused, or recycled. The most common forms in healthcare are:

  • Energy from Waste (EfW) — offensive waste sent to facilities that generate electricity or heat from combustion
  • Anaerobic Digestion (AD) — organic/food waste converted to biogas and digestate
  • Alternative Treatment (AT) — autoclaving or other treatment that renders clinical waste safe for subsequent energy recovery

Tier 5: Disposal

Disposal — particularly High Temperature Incineration (HTI) without energy recovery or landfill — is the least preferred option. It represents a complete loss of the material's value and, in the case of HTI, generates significant carbon emissions. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy explicitly targets reducing HTI volumes as a core sustainability goal.

The Scale of the Challenge in Healthcare

590,000tonnes of total waste produced by the NHS per year (all waste streams)
156,000tonnes of clinical waste from NHS secondary care annually (ERIC 2020/21)
100,000tonnes CO₂e carbon impact of NHS clinical waste each year
£11mestimated annual saving from implementing the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy

The NHS is, by any measure, a colossal waste producer. In 2016/17, the NHS produced approximately 590,000 tonnes of waste — representing around 2% of all commercial waste in England. Of that, approximately 156,000 tonnes are classified as clinical waste requiring specialist treatment.

The carbon impact is equally striking: the NHS's own Clinical Waste Strategy estimates that clinical waste management generates approximately 100,000 tonnes of CO₂e per year. This is not a marginal issue — it is one of the significant contributors to the NHS carbon footprint, alongside travel and procurement.

Important: Waste volumes have been growing at approximately 3% per annum pre-COVID. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy projects that without intervention, total clinical and offensive waste will rise from approximately 149,000 tonnes in 2019/20 to an estimated 200,000 tonnes by 2029/30. Action to reduce, reuse and divert waste is not optional — it is essential.

Why Healthcare Is Different: The Infection Control Tension

Here is the central challenge you will encounter in every aspect of this module: healthcare waste management sits at the intersection of two imperatives that can pull in opposite directions — infection control and sustainability.

In most sectors, the waste hierarchy can be applied relatively straightforwardly. In healthcare, however, every decision must be evaluated against its infection control implications. Consider these examples:

Situation Sustainability preference Infection control constraint Resolution
Sharps containers Reuse containers after emptying Must ensure no cross-contamination between cycles Validated decontamination process required (e.g., Sharpak Zero™ model)
Surgical gowns Reusable textile gowns (reuse tier) Must meet same barrier protection standards as single-use EN 13795 compliant reusable gowns; industrial laundering to validated cycles
PVC IV bags Recycle clean PVC waste Bags contaminated with medication or bodily fluids cannot enter recycling Strict source segregation of clean/uncontaminated items only
Food waste Anaerobic digestion (recovery tier) Food waste in clinical areas may be contaminated Separate food waste streams in ward kitchens and staff areas from clinical zones

This tension does not make the waste hierarchy inapplicable — but it requires that you apply it with clinical intelligence, not mechanically. The hierarchy tells you the order of preference; infection control tells you the conditions under which each tier is permissible.

👨‍🏫

One of the most common mistakes I see in healthcare waste management is people treating the infection control concern as an automatic veto on any sustainability initiative. "We can't reuse that because of infection risk" is sometimes valid — but it is often used as a default objection when the real issue is lack of validated protocols or staff training. Your job as a waste manager is to scrutinise those objections: is the infection risk real and documented, or is it a perceived risk based on habit? The evidence base for reusable sharps containers, for instance, is robust and well-established.

Waste Segregation at Source: The Foundation of Everything

Before the waste hierarchy can be applied, waste must be correctly identified and segregated at the point of generation. This is not merely administrative — correct segregation at source is the single most important act in the entire waste management chain. Here is why:

  • A recyclable item placed in a clinical (orange bag) waste stream cannot later be diverted to recycling — it is now contaminated waste requiring expensive treatment
  • An offensive waste item incorrectly placed in a yellow (HTI) bag incurs 4–5 times the disposal cost of correct classification, with no benefit to patient safety
  • A hazardous item incorrectly disposed of as offensive waste creates compliance failure, potential prosecution, and environmental harm

The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy's flagship compliance target reflects this directly: by 2026, all NHS providers are required to achieve a 20:20:60 waste segregation split — 20% to high temperature incineration, 20% to alternative treatment, and 60% to the offensive waste stream. Achieving this requires not just policy, but sustained staff training, clear signage, and waste auditing.

Key Principle: Waste segregation is not just a compliance obligation — it is the mechanism through which the waste hierarchy becomes practically achievable. If waste is not segregated correctly, higher-tier options (recycling, reuse) become impossible and you are forced down to lower tiers (disposal) by default.

The 20:20:60 Target in Detail

It is worth spending a moment understanding the significance of the 20:20:60 target, because it operationalises the waste hierarchy in a very direct way.

Current position: Research consistently shows that many NHS trusts are currently sending far more waste to HTI and AT than clinically necessary — because clinical and offensive waste is being co-mingled. Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, for example, reported that only around 18% of its clinical waste was correctly segregated as offensive (tiger bag) waste against a best-practice benchmark of 60%. Achieving correct segregation at that trust is estimated to save approximately £100,000 per year and 515 tonnes of CO₂e.

Think about what this means in waste hierarchy terms. Offensive waste — items like incontinence products, non-infectious wound dressings, and hygiene waste — has no hazardous properties. It does not need HTI. It can be sent to energy-from-waste facilities, which is a higher tier (recovery) than HTI disposal. By miscategorising offensive waste as clinical waste, trusts are:

  • Spending 4–5× more on disposal than necessary
  • Generating unnecessary carbon emissions from energy-intensive treatment
  • Missing the opportunity to recover energy via more efficient EfW routes

HTM 07-01: The Technical Framework

The legal framework of the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011 is given practical operational expression through Health Technical Memorandum 07-01: Safe and sustainable management of healthcare waste. This is the definitive technical guidance for healthcare waste management in England, published by NHS England and updated regularly.

HTM 07-01 Health Technical Memorandum 07-01: Safe and sustainable management of healthcare waste. The principal technical guidance document for healthcare waste management in England, covering waste classification, segregation, handling, storage, transport and disposal. It is the operational complement to the legislative framework of the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011.

The most recent revision of HTM 07-01 places explicit emphasis on the environmental benefits of correct waste management and presents opportunities for cost savings, safer working practices, and reduced carbon emissions. It is essential reading for anyone working in healthcare waste management at any level.

Compliance note: HTM 07-01 is technical guidance, not statutory legislation. However, failure to follow it can constitute evidence of non-compliance with the Environmental Protection Act 1990, the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005, and the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011. In practice, regulators — including the Environment Agency — will refer to HTM 07-01 when investigating incidents or issuing enforcement notices.

Lesson Summary: Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways from Lesson 1:
  • The waste hierarchy — prevent, prepare for reuse, recycle, recover, dispose — is a legal duty under the Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011, transposing Article 4 of the Waste Framework Directive
  • The NHS produces approximately 590,000 tonnes of total waste and 156,000 tonnes of clinical waste per year, with an estimated 100,000 tonnes CO₂e carbon impact
  • Healthcare applies the hierarchy under a unique tension: infection control obligations can restrict higher-tier options, but this must be evidence-based, not reflexive
  • Correct waste segregation at source is the foundation of applying the hierarchy — without it, higher-tier options are foreclosed
  • The NHS 20:20:60 segregation target (20% HTI, 20% AT, 60% offensive waste) by 2026 directly operationalises the hierarchy
  • HTM 07-01 provides the operational framework for implementing these obligations in practice

Reflection Questions

  1. Think about your own workplace (or a healthcare setting you are familiar with). At which tier of the waste hierarchy do you think most waste currently ends up? What barriers exist to moving it up the hierarchy?
  2. Article 4(2) of the Waste Framework Directive allows departure from the hierarchy "where this is justified by life-cycle thinking." Can you think of a specific healthcare waste stream where this might apply, and why?
  3. If you had to explain the importance of the 20:20:60 target to a ward nurse who had never heard of it, how would you frame it in terms they would find personally relevant?
Practical Exercise: Obtain a copy of the NHS Clinical Waste Strategy (published March 2023 by NHS England) and identify the six strategic priority areas. For each area, note the specific measure of success and target date. Consider how these six areas map onto the five tiers of the waste hierarchy — which tiers receive the most policy attention, and which are relatively neglected?

lessons.title: Key Concepts: Prevention and Reduction in Healthcare
lessons.content:
The Priority at the Top of the Pyramid

In Lesson 1, you learned that the waste hierarchy places prevention at the very top — the most desirable outcome of all. And yet, in most healthcare organisations, prevention receives the least attention. Resources are concentrated on managing the waste that already exists: collecting it, treating it, disposing of it. This is understandable, but it is also a strategic error. In this lesson, you will learn why prevention should be your first priority, how to achieve it in a healthcare context, and what the quantified evidence tells us about its potential.

590,000tonnes of total NHS waste per year — 15–25% could be prevented through targeted interventions
88,500–147,500tonnes/year potentially preventable with current best practice
£11mestimated annual revenue savings from improved NHS waste management including prevention
The prevention imperative: Research consistently indicates that between 15 and 25% of NHS waste could be prevented through better procurement, product substitution, inventory management, and supply chain engagement. At the NHS's current total waste volume of approximately 590,000 tonnes per year, this represents a preventable waste burden of between 88,500 and 147,500 tonnes annually — before a single bin is emptied or a single vehicle departs.

What Waste Prevention Actually Means

Prevention, in the legal sense defined by the Waste Framework Directive, means measures taken before a substance or material has become waste. This includes measures that:

  • Reduce the quantity of waste generated (including through the reuse of products)
  • Reduce the adverse impacts of the waste generated on the environment and human health
  • Reduce the content of harmful substances in materials and products

In healthcare, prevention is therefore broader than simply "using less." It encompasses product design, procurement decisions, stock management, clinical practice review, and supply chain partnerships. Let us examine each of these in turn.

1. Procurement Strategies: Buying Less Waste Into the System

Every item purchased by a healthcare organisation represents potential future waste. The procurement stage is the single best moment to prevent waste — once a product is on the ward, the opportunity for prevention is largely lost.

Packaging Rationalisation

Pharmaceutical and medical device suppliers often apply excessive or unnecessary packaging. In a single acute hospital, this can represent tonnes of cardboard, plastic film, and packing material per year. Effective procurement prevention strategies include:

  • Bulk purchasing and dispensing: Purchasing medications in bulk containers rather than individual blister packs or sachets, where clinically appropriate, dramatically reduces packaging waste at source
  • Packaging specifications in tender criteria: Including packaging minimisation requirements in procurement tenders, and scoring suppliers accordingly in procurement evaluation frameworks
  • Direct delivery models: Working with suppliers on transit packaging reduction — specifying returnable containers and reusable packaging for high-volume regular deliveries
  • Eliminating unnecessary secondary packaging: Many medical devices arrive in multiple layers of packaging; working with suppliers to reduce secondary and tertiary packaging where primary sterile integrity is maintained
Example — Procedure pack rationalisation: A large NHS trust undertaking an audit of its procedure packs found that many packs contained items that were consistently unused in the clinical procedure for which they were designed. By working with the pack supplier to redesign packs, removing consistently unused items, the trust reduced pack weight and material content by approximately 20% — without any change to clinical outcomes. The waste reduction was achieved entirely through procurement redesign, before the packs ever reached the ward.

Sustainable Procurement Policy

The NHS Sustainable Development Strategy requires NHS organisations to embed sustainability considerations — including waste reduction — into procurement decisions. Under the Social Value Act 2012 and NHS procurement frameworks, organisations are expected to consider whole-life environmental impacts when awarding contracts. This is not merely aspirational: it is a policy expectation that you, as a waste manager, can leverage when engaging procurement colleagues.

2. Product Substitution: Single-Use to Reusable

The shift from single-use to reusable products is one of the most impactful prevention and reuse strategies available to healthcare organisations. It operates simultaneously at Tier 1 (prevention — fewer items to dispose of) and Tier 2 (preparing for reuse) of the waste hierarchy.

Before we explore specific examples, it is important to address the standard objection head-on: the concern that reusable items compromise infection control. As you will see, where validated protocols exist, the evidence does not support this concern — and the environmental and financial gains are substantial.

Reusable Sharps Containers

Single-use sharps containers represent one of the largest volumes of avoidable plastic waste generated by clinical settings. Every ward, every theatre, every clinical room generates sharps containers that are used once and sent for high temperature incineration. The NHS Clinical Waste Strategy explicitly identifies increased use of reusable sharps containers as a key sustainability action.

Reusable sharps container evidence: Services using systems such as Sharpak Zero™ reusable sharps containers have demonstrated carbon savings of approximately 87% per container lifetime compared to single-use alternatives. A trial at Hammersmith Hospital (Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust) across two ICU units saved 1.26 tonnes of CO₂e in just eight weeks. The containers are collected, emptied under controlled conditions, sanitised, inspected, and returned to service — with the contents (sharps) sent for HTI, not the container.

The key insight here is the decoupling of the clinical waste from the container. The sharps themselves must still be managed through HTI — that clinical risk does not change. But the plastic container does not need to be destroyed; it can be reused many times. This is an elegant application of Tier 2 (preparing for reuse) that saves both cost and carbon without any compromise to clinical safety.

Surgical Gowns and Drapes
