A more targeted approach is to conduct them following composition audit findings that suggest a problem, or following staff training to measure whether behaviour has changed.

Practical Example: A community hospital conducts a composition audit of its yellow-lidded sharps containers from a busy treatment room. They find that 22% of the container contents are non-sharp items — packaging wrappers, cotton wool, and gloves. This finding has two implications: (1) the containers are being used as general clinical waste receptacles rather than exclusively for sharps, increasing sharps injury risk during collection; and (2) the organisation is paying sharps disposal rates for items that could go into a yellow bag. This finding would never emerge from a compliance audit alone.

Type 3: The System Audit

System Audit A review of the management systems, documentation, training records, and organisational arrangements that underpin waste management — examining whether the infrastructure for good practice is in place, rather than the practice itself.

The system audit asks: "Do we have the right structures, processes, and people in place to manage waste well — and can we prove it?" It sits at a higher level of abstraction than the other two types. Rather than looking at what is in a bin or whether a specific procedure is being followed today, it examines whether the organisation has built the foundations that make sustained good performance possible.

A system audit examines areas such as:

  • Training and competency records: Is there documented evidence that staff have received waste awareness training? Is training refreshed at appropriate intervals (HTM 07-01 recommends at least annually for clinical staff)? Are training records maintained and accessible?
  • Policy currency: Has the waste management policy been reviewed and approved within the last 2–3 years? Does it reflect current legislation and guidance? Is it accessible to staff?
  • Procedure documentation: Are Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) in place for key waste management tasks — waste segregation, internal collection, storage area management, emergency spill response? Are they version-controlled?
  • Waste contractor oversight: Is there a documented process for verifying the environmental permits and carrier licences of waste contractors? Are contractor performance reviews occurring?
  • Incident recording: Are waste-related incidents (spillages, near-misses, segregation failures, sharps injuries) being recorded, investigated, and used to drive improvement?
  • Management review: Is waste management a standing agenda item at an appropriate governance forum? Are audit findings being formally reviewed and signed off?

The system audit is the type most closely aligned with ISO 14001 internal audit requirements, and it is the type that will be most scrutinised by certification bodies and regulators examining your overall management maturity.

Typical frequency: Full system audits are typically conducted annually, often timed to align with the organisation's broader clinical governance or environmental management review cycle.

How the Three Types Work Together

It is essential to understand that these three audit types are not alternatives — they are complementary. Each reveals a different dimension of your waste management performance, and together they give you a complete picture.

Audit Type Primary Question Evidence Generated Typical Frequency Key Standards Reference
Compliance Audit Are we following the rules? Conformance/non-conformance against legislative and policy criteria Annual (minimum) EPA 1990 s.34, HTM 07-01 Chapter 6, Hazardous Waste Regs 2005
Composition Audit What is actually in our bins? Quantified data on waste stream composition and segregation accuracy Bi-annual or annual HTM 07-01 Chapter 6, waste minimisation principles
System Audit Do we have the right infrastructure? Assessment of management systems, training records, documentation, governance Annual ISO 14001:2015 Clause 9.2, HTM 07-01 Chapter 6

Think of it this way: a compliance audit might confirm that your sharps containers are correctly labelled and in the right locations. A composition audit might then reveal that those correctly positioned containers contain a disproportionate amount of non-sharp waste. A system audit might then uncover the root cause: training records show that bank nurses — who provide 40% of ward nursing hours — have never received waste segregation training because the training system only captures substantive staff.

No single audit type would have surfaced the full picture. Together, they paint it clearly.

👨‍🏫

In my experience, organisations that struggle with waste management improvement almost always fall into one of two traps. The first is conducting only compliance audits — they tick the boxes but never look inside the bags, so they never discover the 40% mis-segregation rate that is costing them £80,000 a year. The second trap is conducting composition audits without the system audit dimension — they find the problem, but have no idea whether their training system, their SOPs, or their governance arrangements are capable of sustaining any improvement they make. Build all three into your programme, and you will have genuine management intelligence rather than isolated snapshots.

The Financial and Environmental Case

While the regulatory obligation to audit is reason enough, it is worth being explicit about the financial and environmental drivers, because these are often what secure genuine organisational commitment to a robust audit programme.

£500–£800 Typical cost per tonne for clinical waste incineration (high-temperature treatment)
£80–£120 Typical cost per tonne for offensive/hygiene waste disposal (non-clinical landfill or autoclave route)

The gap between those two figures explains why correct segregation — and auditing to verify it — is a direct financial lever. A medium-sized district general hospital generating 500 tonnes of yellow bag waste per year, with a 40% mis-segregation rate (items that should be offensive or domestic waste), is disposing of 200 tonnes of incorrectly classified waste at clinical rates. At a conservative £400/tonne differential, that is £80,000 of avoidable annual expenditure. A composition audit that surfaces this finding, followed by targeted staff training and system improvements, can deliver a return on investment within months.

The environmental argument is equally compelling. Clinical waste incineration generates CO₂, NOₓ, and other emissions. Every tonne of non-infectious waste incorrectly incinerated as clinical waste represents avoidable emissions. As healthcare organisations increasingly report against net-zero commitments — NHS England's Net Zero target is 2040 for direct emissions — waste composition data becomes part of your sustainability reporting infrastructure.

Key Takeaway: Waste auditing is simultaneously a compliance obligation, a cost reduction tool, and a sustainability mechanism. Presenting it to senior management in all three framings is far more effective than presenting it as a regulatory requirement alone.

Try This: Mapping Your Current Audit Landscape

Reflective Exercise — 15 minutes

Before moving to the next lesson, take a few minutes to map your organisation's current audit landscape. For each of the three audit types, ask yourself:

  1. Does your organisation currently conduct this type of audit? If yes, how frequently? If no, is there an identified gap?
  2. Who is responsible for conducting it? Is that responsibility formally documented?
  3. Where are the results reported — which governance forum or management body reviews the findings?
  4. Can you point to documented evidence of corrective actions taken in response to the last audit findings?

If you find yourself answering "I don't know" or "I'm not sure" to more than one of these questions, that itself is a finding. You have just conducted the first ten minutes of a system audit.

Key Takeaways

☐HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 and EPA 1990 s.34 establish auditing as a legal and regulatory expectation, not a discretionary activity
☐The compliance audit measures conformance with legislation, guidance, and policy — it tells you whether you are following the rules
☐The waste composition audit physically examines waste stream contents — it tells you what is actually going into your bins
☐The system audit examines management infrastructure — training, documentation, governance, contractor oversight
☐All three types are complementary — no single type provides complete management intelligence
☐Waste auditing has a direct financial return through identifying over-classification and avoidable disposal costs
☐ISO 14001:2015 Clause 9.2 requires internal environmental audits at planned intervals for certified organisations

Reflection Questions

  1. Why might an organisation that conducts thorough compliance audits still have persistently high mis-segregation rates in its clinical areas? What type of audit would surface the underlying causes?
  2. A colleague argues that "auditing is unnecessary bureaucracy — we have good training and experienced staff." How would you respond, drawing on both the regulatory framework and the practical evidence from composition audit data?
  3. Consider a healthcare setting you are familiar with (or your current workplace). Which of the three audit types do you believe would generate the most immediately actionable findings, and why?

lessons.title: Key Concepts: Planning and Preparing for the Audit
lessons.content:
Failing to Plan Is Planning to Fail

An audit that has not been properly planned will, at best, generate incomplete data. At worst, it will generate unsafe conditions for the auditors, miss critical areas, and produce findings that cannot be defended when challenged by management or regulators. Good audit planning is not administrative overhead — it is what separates an audit that drives genuine improvement from one that generates a report that sits in a filing cabinet.

In this lesson, we are going to work through the full planning and preparation process for a healthcare waste audit, step by step. By the end, you will have a complete framework you can adapt for your own organisation, regardless of its size or the type of audit you are conducting.

Lesson Objectives: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to (1) define an appropriate scope for a waste audit; (2) identify the competencies required in an audit team; (3) develop a comprehensive audit checklist aligned to regulatory standards; (4) apply correct health and safety measures for auditors working with healthcare waste; and (5) produce a structured audit plan and schedule.

Step 1: Defining the Audit Scope

Every audit begins with a scope statement. The scope defines three things: which areas you will audit, which waste streams you will examine, and which time period your evidence will cover. Getting the scope right is critical — too broad, and the audit becomes unmanageable; too narrow, and the findings lack statistical and operational significance.

Defining Areas

In a healthcare setting, "areas" typically means clinical departments, support services, or physical locations. You need to make a deliberate decision about which areas to include, and document the rationale. There are several legitimate approaches:

  • Risk-based selection: Prioritise areas that generate the highest volumes of clinical waste, areas where previous audits have identified problems, or areas with high staff turnover (and therefore potentially lower training retention). This is the most defensible approach for organisations with limited audit resource.
  • Rotating coverage: Divide your clinical areas into a rolling programme — covering all areas over a 2-year cycle, with higher-risk areas audited more frequently. This ensures comprehensive coverage without overwhelming your audit capacity.
  • Whole-organisation audit: For a full compliance or system audit, you may need to cover all areas simultaneously. This requires a larger team and more logistics, but gives you a complete organisational picture.

Important: Never exclude an area from the scope simply because you expect to find problems there. The purpose of an audit is to surface the reality of current performance, not to confirm that everything is fine. Auditing only areas you believe are compliant produces misleading results and misses the improvement opportunities that matter most.

Defining Waste Streams

Specify which waste streams you will examine. In healthcare, the principal streams are:

  • Infectious clinical waste (yellow bags — Category B)
  • Offensive/hygiene waste (tiger stripe bags)
  • Sharps — infectious (yellow-lidded containers)
  • Sharps — non-infectious/medicinal (purple-lidded containers)
  • Anatomical/pathological waste (red bags)
  • Cytotoxic/cytostatic waste (purple bags/containers)
  • Pharmaceutical waste
  • Domestic/general waste
  • Recycling streams (paper, cardboard, glass, mixed dry)
  • Confidential waste
  • Dental waste (amalgam, x-ray fixer, lead foil)

For a composition audit, you will physically examine a selection of these streams. For a compliance audit, you will examine the management arrangements for all streams within scope. Be explicit in your scope document about which streams are included and which, if any, are excluded with justification.

Defining the Time Period

The time period of your evidence review is particularly important for compliance and system audits. Specify the period for which you will review documentation — for example, "Waste transfer notes for the 12 months preceding the audit date." For composition audits, the time period is less relevant (you are examining current waste), but you should specify the audit dates clearly so findings can be contextualised (for example, a post-Christmas audit may show atypical domestic waste volumes in clinical areas).

Template — Scope Statement: "This audit covers [audit type(s)] of healthcare waste management across [named areas/all areas] of [organisation name]. The waste streams within scope are [list streams]. Documentation review will cover the period [start date] to [audit date]. The following areas/streams are excluded from this audit: [list exclusions and reasons, or state 'none']."

Step 2: Assembling the Audit Team

The composition of your audit team has a direct bearing on the quality and credibility of your findings. Different audit types require different competencies, and a well-assembled team brings complementary perspectives that an individual auditor cannot provide.

Core Competencies for Healthcare Waste Auditors

Competency Why It Matters Relevant Audit Types
Knowledge of healthcare waste legislation (EPA 1990, Hazardous Waste Regs, HTM 07-01) Cannot identify non-compliance without knowing what compliance looks like Compliance, System
Practical knowledge of waste stream classification Essential for correctly identifying mis-classified waste during composition audit Composition
Data recording and analytical skills Audit data must be recorded accurately and consistently; errors undermine findings All types
Health and safety awareness (PPE, infection control, sharps handling) Auditors are exposed to healthcare waste; risk must be managed rigorously Composition especially
Interpersonal and communication skills Staff interviews are part of compliance and system audits; non-confrontational approach needed Compliance, System
Organisational independence Auditors should not audit areas they directly manage — independence protects objectivity All types

Team Composition in Practice

For most internal audits in a medium-to-large healthcare organisation, an effective team consists of:

  • Lead Auditor (1 person): The Waste Manager, Estates and Facilities professional, or an individual with a waste management qualification. Responsible for directing the audit, making classification judgements, and producing the report.
  • Technical Auditor (1–2 people): May include infection control personnel (invaluable for clinical waste classification decisions), a sustainability lead, or a trained waste champion from a non-audited area.
  • Data Recorder (1 person): Dedicated to recording weights, observations, and photographic evidence during composition audits. Separating the recording role from the sorting/classification role reduces errors significantly.

For system and compliance audits, consider including a representative from clinical governance or risk management. Their involvement adds credibility to findings and makes it easier to escalate corrective actions through governance channels.

👨‍🏫

One of the most valuable team members for a healthcare waste audit is an experienced infection control nurse or practitioner. They bring clinical credibility when discussing findings with ward staff, they can make authoritative judgements about whether waste should be classified as infectious, and their presence signals to clinical staff that waste management is a patient safety and infection control issue — not just an Estates and Facilities concern. If you can secure their involvement, even for part of the audit, it will strengthen both the process and the reception of your findings.

Step 3: Developing the Audit Checklist

The audit checklist is your structured observation guide — it translates your regulatory and policy requirements into specific, observable, verifiable criteria. A well-designed checklist ensures consistency across auditors and areas, makes findings directly traceable to the standards they reference, and enables straightforward scoring and reporting.

Structuring Your Checklist

Organise your checklist by theme or audit type, and for each criterion, identify: (1) what you are looking for, (2) the standard or requirement it derives from, (3) how you will verify it (observation, document review, staff interview), and (4) the severity if non-compliant (critical, major, minor).

Sample Compliance Audit Checklist Criteria

☐Segregation at source: Are clinical waste bags/containers present at appropriate clinical points? Are they the correct colour and type? (HTM 07-01 Annex A) — Verify by observation
☐Container labelling: Are clinical waste containers labelled with department/ward of origin and date sealed? (HTM 07-01 para 6.12) — Verify by observation
☐Sharps containers: Are sharps containers ¾-full maximum before sealing? Are they not overfilled? (HTM 07-01, UN3291 requirements) — Verify by observation and staff interview
☐Waste transfer documentation: Are waste transfer notes (WTNs) in place for the current and preceding 2 years? Are they completed with all required fields? (EPA 1990 s.34, Environmental Protection (Duty of Care) Regs 1991) — Verify by document review
☐Carrier licences: Does your waste contractor hold a current Environment Agency waste carrier registration? Is it verified and recorded? (Waste (England and Wales) Regulations 2011) — Verify by document review
☐Storage area: Is the clinical waste storage area locked, appropriately signed (biohazard), ventilated, and pest-resistant? (HTM 07-01 Chapter 5) — Verify by inspection
☐Collection frequency: Is clinical waste collected within the maximum storage periods (5 days for Category B, immediate for Category A)? (HTM 07-01 para 5.16) — Verify by collection schedule and observation
☐Staff training: Do training records confirm that relevant staff have received waste management training within the last 12 months? (HTM 07-01 Chapter 7) — Verify by document review

Severity Classification: Use a three-tier classification for findings: Critical — immediate risk to safety, legal non-compliance (e.g., sharps container overfilled; no WTNs for 18 months); Major — significant non-conformance with potential for harm or regulatory breach (e.g., waste storage area unlocked; training records 18 months overdue); Minor — lower-risk non-conformance or procedural deviation (e.g., one bin in wrong location; date missing from sealed bag). This classification drives the prioritisation and timescales of corrective actions.

Step 4: Notification Requirements

Before you arrive in a clinical area with an audit checklist and PPE kit, consider who needs to be informed and how much notice to give. This is a balance between two legitimate considerations.

Arguments for pre-notification: Clinical areas are busy environments. Arriving unannounced for a composition audit that requires space to sort waste can disrupt patient care. Pre-notification allows staff to ensure a suitable space is available and that a ward manager or clinical lead is present to facilitate interviews and access to documentation.

Arguments against pre-notification (or for minimal notice): If staff know an audit is coming two weeks in advance, they may clean up areas, improve segregation temporarily, or locate missing documentation. This gives you a picture of best-case performance rather than typical performance — which is exactly what you do not want.

The practical resolution that most experienced auditors use is partial notification: inform ward managers and area leads that an audit will occur during a specified week, but do not give advance notice of specific days or times. This respects operational needs without enabling the "audit effect" that inflates apparent compliance.

Note on Sensitivity: Some areas will push back on unannounced auditing, arguing that it undermines trust. Frame the audit positively: "We are conducting this audit to help identify where additional support or resource is needed — not to catch people out." This framing is both honest and strategically sound. Your goal is improvement, not blame.

Step 5: Health and Safety for Auditors

This section is non-negotiable. Anyone involved in a healthcare waste audit — and particularly a composition audit — is exposed to a range of hazards that must be systematically managed before the audit begins. The audit team lead is responsible for ensuring that an appropriate risk assessment is in place and that all team members have been briefed.

PPE Requirements for Composition Audits

Hazard Required PPE Specification
Sharps injury risk Puncture-resistant gloves EN388:2016 certified, puncture resistance level 4 minimum. Do NOT use standard latex or nitrile examination gloves — these provide no puncture protection. Use cut/puncture resistant gloves over a nitrile inner glove
Forearm puncture/laceration from bag contents Long-sleeve arm protection Chain mail or cut-resistant sleeves worn over a disposable gown; puncture-resistant gloves should extend to mid-forearm minimum
Biological contamination/infection risk Disposable gown or coverall, nitrile inner gloves Type 6 disposable coverall or disposable apron and gown; fluid-resistant. Change between areas to prevent cross-contamination
Splash from bag contents Eye protection, face shield Sealed eye protection (goggles) or full-face shield — safety spectacles are insufficient
Inhalation of airborne pathogens or particulates Respiratory protection FFP2 mask as minimum; FFP3 if dealing with waste from respiratory isolation areas
Foot injury from fallen sharps containers Safety footwear Steel-toecap, puncture-resistant sole (EN ISO 20345 S3 standard)

Sharps Injury Protocol

Despite all precautions, sharps injuries remain a risk during composition audits. Every audit team member must be briefed on the sharps injury response protocol before the audit begins — not when it happens. Ensure the following is in place:

☐First aid kit with wound management supplies must be immediately accessible in the audit working area
☐All audit team members should know the location of the nearest occupational health or emergency department
☐Sharps injury response poster must be visible in the working area (encourage bleeding, do not suck, wash thoroughly, report immediately)
☐Infection control must be contactable during the audit — their input may be needed immediately following a sharps injury to assess post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) requirement
☐All team members should have current hepatitis B vaccination — check before the audit date, not on it

Critical Safety Rule: Never hand-sort sharps. If you encounter sharps containers during a composition audit, do not open them. Record their presence, weigh them if appropriate, and set them aside. The only waste items you should be manually handling during a composition audit are the contents of soft-pack waste bags — and even then, use tongs or forceps for any items that cannot be safely identified visually before contact.

Manual Handling

Waste bags in clinical areas can be heavy — particularly in areas generating high volumes of single-use equipment. Your risk assessment should address manual handling: use appropriate trolleys or bin transfer equipment where bags exceed 10kg, and ensure team members have received manual handling training. Do not drag bags across floors — this can cause tears and contamination.

Working in Pairs

A composition audit must never be conducted by a lone worker. The minimum team for any physical waste examination is two people — one to sort and classify, one to record and observe. This is a safety requirement (the observer can respond immediately to a sharps injury or contamination incident) and a quality requirement (a second observer significantly improves the consistency of classification decisions).

Step 6: The Audit Plan and Schedule

Consolidate your planning decisions into a formal audit plan document. This serves as your operational guide, your notification document for area managers, and your evidence of a planned and systematic approach (which your ISO 14001 auditor will want to see).

Audit Plan Template

Audit Reference: [e.g., WA-2026-04]

Audit Type(s): [Compliance / Composition / System — or combination]

Organisation / Site:

Lead Auditor:

Audit Team Members:

Audit Scope — Areas:

Audit Scope — Waste Streams:

Documentation Review Period:

Audit Dates:

Standards and References: [EPA 1990 s.34; HTM 07-01 Chapters 4, 5, 6; Hazardous Waste Regs 2005; ISO 14001:2015 Cl.9.2]

PPE and Safety Arrangements:

Equipment Required: [weighing scales, sample bags, forceps, tongs, data sheets, camera/phone for evidence photos, audit checklist, PPE kit]

Notification: [Who has been informed, method of notification, date]

Report Due Date:

Report Recipients:

Scheduling the Audit

Consider timing carefully. Avoid auditing clinical areas during very high-activity periods (Monday mornings in A&E, for example) where your presence and activities could obstruct patient care. However, do not audit areas during known low-activity periods if you want a representative picture — a ward at 8pm with minimal staffing will not show you what the segregation behaviour is like during a busy morning round.

For composition audits, consider the day of the week. A sample of waste taken on a Friday afternoon will include waste accumulated across the full week, giving you a more representative cross-section than a Monday-morning sample that only reflects the previous evening's activity.

Key Takeaways

☐Audit scope must define areas, waste streams, and time period — and the rationale for scope decisions should be documented
☐Audit team competencies must match the audit type — include infection control expertise for composition audits
☐Audit checklists must be traceable to specific standards and regulations — every criterion needs a source reference
☐Partial notification (week-level, not day-level) balances operational respect with authentic performance measurement
☐PPE for composition audits must include puncture-resistant gloves (EN388), arm protection, face shield, disposable coverall, FFP2 respirator, and safety footwear
☐Sharps injury protocol must be briefed before the audit begins — all team members must know their response procedure
☐Composition audits must never be conducted by a lone worker — two-person minimum at all times

Reflection Questions

  1. You are planning a composition audit of your organisation's yellow bag waste. What factors would you consider when deciding which areas to include in the scope, and how would you document the rationale for any areas you exclude?
  2. A ward manager tells you she is not happy with staff being "interrupted" by an unannounced audit. How would you handle this conversation, and how would you balance her concerns with the requirement to obtain authentic performance data?
  3. Review the PPE requirements table in this lesson. Identify any gaps in your current personal understanding of PPE selection — and note them as a training need to follow up before conducting your first composition audit.

lessons.title: Regulations: Conducting the Waste Composition Audit
lessons.content:
From Observation to Evidence: The Science of Waste Composition Analysis

The waste composition audit is, in many respects, the most technically demanding of the three audit types. It requires physical interaction with potentially hazardous materials, disciplined adherence to a sampling protocol, precise data recording, and sound judgement about waste classification. When conducted correctly, it produces the most objectively powerful evidence available to a waste manager — quantified, reproducible data that can directly drive financial and behavioural change.

In this lesson, we are going to work through the full methodology, step by step. By the end, you will understand exactly what is required at every stage of the process, why each step matters for data validity, and how to make sound classification decisions under the sometimes-ambiguous conditions of real waste examination.

Lesson Objectives: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to (1) select statistically representative waste samples; (2) apply the correct weighing and sorting protocol; (3) classify waste items accurately using HTM 07-01 criteria; (4) record composition data to the required standard; and (5) calculate percentage composition and mis-segregation rates.

The Sampling Protocol: Why Three Bags Minimum

Before you open a single bag, you need to select your samples correctly. This is where many informal waste audits go wrong — they examine one or two bags from an area and draw conclusions from that. A single bag is not a representative sample: it reflects the practice of whoever generated that specific bag, at that specific time. You need a minimum sample size to draw valid conclusions about area-wide behaviour.

HTM 07-01 and established NHS audit methodology specify a minimum of three bags per waste stream per area. This minimum reflects the basic statistical principle that variability between individual bags is high (a bag from a Monday morning treatment session will have very different composition from a bag from a Friday afternoon), and that three samples begin to smooth out individual variation. Where possible, increase to five or six samples per stream per area — the larger the sample, the more robust your findings.

Sampling Guidance: Select bags randomly — do not pick the smallest, neatest, or most accessible bags. If bags are numbered or labelled with origin information, use a random selection method (e.g., select the first, middle, and last bag available, or use random number generation if you have many bags to choose from). Document exactly which bags you selected, from which location, and at what time. This traceability is essential if findings are later questioned.

Sampling Across Areas

If you are auditing multiple areas, conduct your sampling in each area on the same day or within the same short period. Composition data from Area A collected in January and compared with Area B data collected in April is not a valid comparison — seasonal variation, different activity levels, and interim training events can all confound the results. Aim to complete all sampling within a 3–5 day window for a multi-area audit.

Representative Timing

As noted in Lesson 2, the timing of sample collection affects representativeness. Ideally, collect samples at a point in the week that reflects average activity — mid-week samples (Tuesday–Thursday) generally provide a more representative cross-section of waste generation than Monday or Friday. Avoid collecting samples immediately after a known training event — you want to measure habitual behaviour, not the immediate effect of a reminder.

Step 1: Weighing the Sealed Bag

Before opening any bag, record its total weight on your data sheet. This is your gross bag weight and serves two purposes: it quantifies the total volume of waste in that stream from that area (useful for waste generation data), and it provides a cross-check for your category weights after sorting (the sum of sorted category weights should equal gross bag weight, minus any weight attributed to the bag itself).

Use calibrated, portable weighing scales — platform scales suitable for weighing bags up to 15–20kg. Do not use kitchen scales or uncalibrated equipment. Record the scale model and calibration date on your data sheet. Calibrated scales are a quality requirement: auditors have been challenged on the accuracy of composition data when they could not demonstrate the calibration status of their equipment.

Data Point When Recorded Purpose
Gross bag weight (sealed) Before opening Total waste volume; cross-check for sorted category weights
Bag origin (area, department, ward) Before opening Traceability; allows area-specific findings
Bag collection date/time Before opening Temporal context; helps interpret composition
Bag type/stream (colour, UN designation) Before opening Confirms which stream is being audited
Category weights (post-sort) After sorting Composition calculation
Photographic evidence reference During and after sort Supports findings; provides visual record
Classification decisions/notes During sort Documents ambiguous items and rationale

Step 2: Preparing the Sorting Area

Before opening any bag, your sorting area must be correctly set up. Do not rush this step. A correctly prepared working area prevents contamination, ensures safety, and enables accurate data recording.

☐Lay an impermeable floor covering (heavy-gauge polythene sheeting or a dedicated leak-proof sorting mat) across the entire working area
☐Set up labelled sorting receptacles (bins or labelled areas on the mat) for each waste category you will sort into
☐Position scales within easy reach, with a clear display visible to both sorter and data recorder
☐Place forceps and tongs within reach — for items that cannot be safely handled directly even with PPE
☐Ensure adequate lighting — you need to visually identify waste items accurately
☐Confirm ventilation is adequate — opening sealed clinical waste bags in a poorly ventilated space is an infection control and occupational health risk
☐Confirm all team members are fully PPE-equipped before proceeding (refer to Lesson 2 PPE table)
☐Confirm sharps injury protocol materials are in place and team is briefed

The working area should be located away from patient care areas and food preparation or handling areas. A loading bay, waste holding room, or outdoor designated area (with appropriate screening and drainage containment) are typical locations.

Step 3: Opening the Bag and Sorting Waste into Categories

Open bags carefully — cut the tie rather than pulling it, and open slowly to avoid aerosol generation from liquid contents. Tip the contents onto the sorting area gradually, not in one rapid pour. Observe the contents before touching anything to identify any visible sharps or potentially hazardous items.

Never hand-sort sharps. If you encounter loose sharps (needles, lancets, scalpel blades, broken glass) within a yellow bag — which is itself a finding (sharps should never be placed loose in soft-pack bags) — do not attempt to pick them up by hand. Use forceps. Record their presence as a significant finding: loose sharps in yellow bags present a serious injury risk to waste handlers throughout the disposal chain.

The Standard Category Framework

Sort waste into these standardised categories. Using consistent categories across every audit enables you to compare findings over time and between areas — do not invent your own categories.

Category Items Included Correct Disposal Route
Infectious clinical waste Items contaminated with blood or body fluids from patients with known or suspected infection; any items from Category B infectious waste scenarios Yellow bag — High Temperature Treatment (HTT) or incineration
Offensive/hygiene waste (non-infectious) Incontinence pads, nappies, sanitary waste, PPE from non-infectious procedures, swabs and dressings from non-infected wounds, non-contaminated single-use items Tiger stripe bag — non-clinical landfill or autoclave
Sharps (infectious) Hypodermic needles, syringes with needles, lancets, scalpel blades from infectious procedures Yellow-lidded sharps container — HTT or incineration
Sharps (non-infectious/medicinal) Needles used only with medicines, insulin syringes (from non-infected patients, community setting) Purple-lidded sharps container — pharmaceutical waste treatment
Pharmaceutical waste Medicines, aerosol inhalers, topical preparations, out-of-date drugs Pharmaceutical waste container — licensed pharmaceutical treatment facility
General domestic waste Uncontaminated packaging, food waste, paper, plastic not contaminated with body fluids or medicines Black/grey bag — domestic waste, recycling where applicable
Recyclable materials Cardboard, paper, uncontaminated plastic, glass (where applicable) Appropriate recycling stream
Anatomical/pathological waste Recognisable human tissue, organs, blood bags Red bag — incineration
Amalgam/dental waste Amalgam capsules, extracted teeth with amalgam restorations, amalgam-contaminated materials Specialist dental waste container — mercury recovery

Making Classification Decisions

Waste classification requires judgement, and that judgement must be applied consistently. The key question for any item in a yellow bag is: "Is this item genuinely infectious clinical waste, or could it safely travel in an alternative, lower-cost stream?"

Under HTM 07-01, infectious clinical waste is waste that: (a) contains or is contaminated with a substance classified as hazardous under the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005 (waste EWC code 18 01 03); or (b) poses a risk of infection to any person coming into contact with it; or (c) has been generated during patient care where infectious status is unknown and precautionary classification is required.

Practically speaking, this means:

  • A glove worn during routine blood pressure monitoring, with no visible blood or body fluid contamination, is not infectious clinical waste — it is offensive/hygiene waste and should travel in a tiger stripe bag
  • A dressing from a non-infected wound with minimal serous exudate is borderline — local policy and clinical context should guide the classification; in the absence of clear infection risk indicators, offensive waste classification is defensible
  • A dressing from a MRSA-positive wound is infectious clinical waste
  • An empty medicine bottle with no liquid residue is general domestic waste or pharmaceutical waste — not clinical waste
  • A plastic tray from a sterile dressing pack that has not contacted body fluids is recyclable waste
👨‍🏫

The most common finding in UK healthcare waste composition audits is the presence of large quantities of offensive/hygiene waste — primarily PPE and non-contaminated single-use items — in yellow clinical waste bags. Staff tend to default to the yellow bag because it is the most visible, most available, and carries the least perceived risk ("if in doubt, yellow bag it"). The problem is that this precautionary approach costs hundreds of thousands of pounds annually across the NHS and generates avoidable emissions. Your composition audit will quantify exactly how significant this behaviour is in your areas — and that quantification is what makes the case for targeted corrective action.

Step 4: Weighing Each Category

Once the bag contents have been sorted into categories, weigh each category and record the weight on your data sheet. Use the same scales you used for the gross bag weight. Weigh sorted items in a clean container (tray, bin) and tare the container weight before weighing.

After weighing all categories, sum the category weights and compare against the gross bag weight (minus the estimated bag film weight, typically 50–100g for a standard clinical waste bag). A discrepancy of more than 5% suggests measurement error or an item missed during sorting — investigate before moving to the next bag.

Step 5: Photographic Evidence

Take systematic photographs at three points in the process:

  1. Sealed bag with identifier — photograph the sealed bag with its label/identifier visible before opening. This confirms the bag's origin and provides a visual record of the sample.
  2. Sorted categories on the mat — after sorting but before weighing, photograph the sorted categories laid out clearly. This is your most important piece of evidence: it shows visually what was in the bag. Include a scale reference (ruler, or labelled objects) if possible.
  3. Specific mis-classified items — photograph any particularly significant findings: loose sharps in a yellow bag, pharmaceuticals in general waste, items of obvious domestic origin in clinical waste.

Number your photographs and cross-reference them with your data sheet. Photographs without traceability to a specific sample are of limited evidential value when reporting findings.

Step 6: Calculating Percentage Composition

Once you have weight data for each category across all bags in your sample for a given area and stream, calculate the percentage composition as follows:

Formula: % of Category X = (Total weight of Category X across all samples ÷ Total gross weight of all samples) × 100

Perform this calculation at stream level (e.g., all yellow bags from Area A) rather than at individual bag level — the area-level figure is more statistically stable and more meaningful for improvement targeting.

Worked Example:
You have audited 4 yellow bags from a medical ward. Gross weights: 1.8kg, 2.2kg, 1.6kg, 2.0kg. Total gross weight: 7.6kg.
After sorting, total offensive/hygiene waste across all 4 bags: 2.85kg
Total genuinely infectious waste: 3.04kg
Total domestic waste: 0.61kg
Total pharmaceuticals: 0.10kg
% Offensive/hygiene in yellow bags = (2.85 ÷ 7.6) × 100 = 37.5%
% Correctly classified infectious = (3.04 ÷ 7.6) × 100 = 40.0%
% Domestic waste in yellow bags = (0.61 ÷ 7.6) × 100 = 8.0%
This means that 60% of the yellow bag weight from this ward does not need to travel at clinical waste rates. The mis-segregation rate — items not correctly classified as infectious — is 60%.

Benchmarking: The 20:20:60 Framework

A useful benchmarking framework widely used in NHS waste management is the 20:20:60 model. In an optimally segregated healthcare setting, the approximate distribution of total waste by weight should be:

20% Clinical/hazardous waste (high-cost disposal)
20% Offensive/hygiene waste (medium-cost disposal)
60% Domestic/recyclable waste (low-cost disposal)

Most healthcare organisations auditing for the first time will find their clinical waste percentage significantly higher than 20% — often 40–50%. This tells you that offensive and domestic waste items are being over-classified into clinical streams. Your composition audit data will show you exactly where the over-classification is occurring and what specific items are driving it.

Statistical Significance Considerations

A brief but important note on the statistical validity of your findings. Three bags per stream per area is a minimum, not an ideal. The more samples you have, the more confident you can be that your percentage composition figures are representative of typical behaviour in that area. With only three bags, your margin of error is wide — a single anomalous bag (for example, a bag from an unusual clinical event) can significantly skew the results.

When reporting your findings, be honest about the limitations of your sample size. Use language that reflects your confidence level: "Based on a sample of [n] bags from [area], the estimated percentage of offensive/hygiene waste in yellow bags is approximately [X]%." Avoid presenting composition percentages as precise, definitive figures when the sample is small. However, do not allow statistical uncertainty to obscure a clear finding — if every single bag in your sample contains 30–40% non-infectious waste, that is a robust finding even with a small sample size.

Data Recording Template: For each bag in your sample, your data sheet should capture: (1) Bag reference number; (2) Area/department; (3) Date and time collected; (4) Waste stream; (5) Gross bag weight; (6) Weight of each sorted category; (7) Observations and classification notes; (8) Photo references; (9) Any safety incidents during sorting.

Post-Audit Waste Disposal

After completing your composition audit, all sorted waste must be correctly re-segregated and disposed of appropriately — not simply returned to the original bags. This is both a legal requirement (you cannot knowingly mis-classify waste you have just identified as non-clinical) and an ethical one. Dispose of each sorted category via its correct route. PPE used during the audit should be disposed of in the appropriate clinical waste stream. The sorting area covering and any contaminated materials should be disposed of as clinical waste.

Key Takeaways

☐Minimum sample size is 3 bags per stream per area — increase to 5–6 where possible for greater statistical confidence
☐Weigh sealed bags before opening and record gross weight — this is essential for composition calculations and cross-checking
☐Use calibrated scales — uncalibrated weighing equipment undermines data validity
☐Sort into standardised categories — do not invent new categories that prevent comparison across audits
☐Never hand-sort sharps — use forceps and record loose sharps in yellow bags as a significant finding
☐Photograph evidence at three points: sealed bag, sorted categories, specific significant findings
☐Calculate composition at stream-area level, not per-bag — area-level figures are more statistically stable
☐Benchmark against 20:20:60 — clinical waste should be ~20% of total waste weight in a well-segregated setting

Reflection Questions

  1. You are auditing a yellow bag from a busy surgical ward and find a blood-stained dressing, three pairs of gloves with no visible contamination, an empty saline bag and giving set, and three pieces of plastic packaging from dressing packs. For each item, identify the correct waste category and explain your reasoning.
  2. Your composition audit of the outpatients department reveals a 55% non-infectious waste rate in yellow bags, based on a sample of three bags. A departmental manager challenges this, arguing that the sample size is too small to draw any conclusions. How do you respond, and what would you do to strengthen the evidence base?
  3. After completing a composition audit, you have sorted waste back into its correct categories. One of your team members suggests putting all the waste back into the yellow bags "because it is easier." How do you respond, and what obligation applies?

lessons.title: Practical Application: Analysing Findings and Writing the Report
lessons.content:
Turning Data Into Action

Collecting audit data is only half the job. The second — and arguably more consequential — half is analysing that data rigorously, understanding what is driving the problems you have found, and communicating your findings in a way that compels action. An audit report that sits on a shared drive unread, or a set of findings presented in a way that makes management defensive rather than engaged, represents a failure of the audit process regardless of how technically sound the fieldwork was.

This lesson covers the full analytical and reporting phase: from calculating non-conformance rates, through root cause analysis, to the structure of the audit report itself and the mechanics of presenting findings to management. We will also look at what makes a corrective action plan genuinely effective — and what makes it a list of aspirations that never get implemented.

Lesson Objectives: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to (1) calculate non-conformance rates from composition and compliance audit data; (2) apply the 5 Whys technique to identify root causes of waste management non-conformances; (3) structure a professional audit report; (4) develop SMART corrective actions; and (5) present findings to management in a way that drives genuine organisational response.

Calculating Non-Conformance Rates

Before you can write a meaningful finding, you need to express it numerically. Qualitative observations ("there was quite a lot of domestic waste in the yellow bags") are less compelling than quantified findings ("domestic waste items constituted 18.4% of yellow bag weight in the outpatient department"). The numerical finding is harder to dismiss, easier to compare over time, and more credible in a management forum.

Compliance Audit Non-Conformance Rate

For a compliance audit conducted with a checklist, calculate the non-conformance rate as:

Non-Conformance Rate (%) = (Number of non-conforming criteria ÷ Total criteria assessed) × 100

Calculate this overall and by theme (e.g., documentation, physical conditions, training, contractor oversight). Theme-level rates help you identify systemic weak points and prioritise resource for improvement.

Also classify findings by severity (Critical, Major, Minor — as defined in Lesson 2). A summary table showing the total count of findings at each severity level, broken down by theme, is usually the most effective single exhibit in a compliance audit report.

Composition Audit Mis-Segregation Rate

Express your composition audit findings in two ways:

  1. Mis-segregation rate by weight: The percentage of yellow (or other clinical) bag weight that should have travelled in a different, lower-cost stream. This is the most financially meaningful figure.
  2. Mis-segregation rate by item count: The percentage of individual items in the sample that were incorrectly classified. This is more useful for staff training — it tells you what proportion of disposal decisions are being made incorrectly.

Worked Example:
Composition audit of yellow bags, Ward 6 (Medical), sample of 5 bags, total gross weight 10.8kg.
Genuinely infectious items: 5.1kg (47.2%) — correctly classified
Offensive/hygiene waste: 3.8kg (35.2%) — should be tiger stripe
Domestic/general waste: 1.5kg (13.9%) — should be black bag
Sharps (loose, not in sharps container): 0.3kg (2.8%) — critical finding, safety risk
Pharmaceuticals: 0.1kg (0.9%) — should be pharmaceutical waste stream
Mis-segregation rate by weight: 52.8% (all non-infectious categories)
This ward is paying clinical waste disposal rates for more than half of its yellow bag weight. If this ward generates 15kg of yellow bag waste per day, that is approximately 8kg/day of incorrectly classified waste — with a cost penalty of approximately £3.30/day (at a differential of £150/tonne), adding up to over £1,200 per year for this ward alone.

Root Cause Analysis: The 5 Whys Technique

Knowing what is wrong is necessary but not sufficient. To design an effective corrective action, you need to understand why it is wrong. The 5 Whys technique is a simple, structured method for moving from a surface-level observation to an underlying root cause.

The technique works by asking "why?" repeatedly — typically five times, though the number varies — until you reach a cause that is genuinely actionable at the organisational level, rather than a proximate symptom.

5 Whys Applied — Example 1:
Finding: 38% of yellow bag weight is offensive/hygiene waste (gloves, non-contaminated packaging)
Why? Staff are placing non-contaminated PPE and packaging into the yellow bag
Why? Staff do not distinguish between offensive/hygiene waste and infectious clinical waste
Why? Staff training does not cover the tiger stripe bag category or the distinction between the two streams
Why? The training module was written in 2018, before tiger stripe bags were introduced at this organisation
Why? There is no process for reviewing waste training content when operational changes (new container types) are introduced
Root Cause: Absence of a change management trigger to update training content when waste management systems change
Corrective Action: (1) Update training module to include tiger stripe bag category; (2) Establish formal process requiring waste team sign-off on training content when waste systems change; (3) Relaunch training to all clinical staff

5 Whys Applied — Example 2:
Finding: Waste transfer notes for hazardous waste are 14 months out of date (should be renewed annually)
Why? The renewal was not actioned when due
Why? There is no diary reminder or renewal alert in the estates management system
Why? The individual responsible left the organisation and their successor was not briefed on the renewal schedule
Why? There is no documented list of regulatory renewal obligations with named ownership and due dates
Root Cause: Regulatory compliance tracking is held informally by individuals rather than in a documented, resilient system
Corrective Action: (1) Immediately renew WTNs; (2) Create a regulatory compliance calendar with all renewal obligations, named owners, and advance reminders; (3) Implement handover procedure for waste compliance responsibilities when staff change roles

Notice how the 5 Whys moves you away from person-blaming ("staff aren't following procedures") toward system-blaming ("the system does not support the right behaviour"). This distinction is critical: corrective actions focused on re-training or telling people to "do better" rarely produce sustained improvement. Actions that change systems, structures, and processes do.

👨‍🏫

The single most common error in waste audit corrective action plans is specifying "re-train all staff" as the sole response to a segregation finding. Training is almost never sufficient on its own, because it does not address the environmental and systemic factors that make incorrect segregation the path of least resistance. Ask yourself: even if everyone received perfect training today, what in the physical environment, the workflow, or the management system might push them back toward incorrect behaviour next week? Your corrective actions need to address those factors — not just the knowledge gap.

Structuring the Audit Report

A professional audit report follows a defined structure. This structure serves multiple purposes: it ensures completeness, it enables readers to navigate directly to the sections most relevant to them, and it provides a document that can be cited in governance records, management reviews, and regulatory inspections.

Standard Audit Report Structure

Section Content Target Reader
1. Executive Summary 1–2 pages maximum. Key headline findings, overall compliance/performance level, top 3–5 priority recommendations. Written for senior management who may not read the full report. Board / Senior leadership
2. Audit Scope and Methodology Scope statement (areas, streams, time period). Audit team. Standards audited against. Methods used (observation, document review, composition sampling). Sample sizes. Dates. Technical reviewers; regulators
3. Findings — Compliance/System Tabulated findings showing criterion, finding, evidence reference, severity classification, and regulatory standard. Group by theme. Include photographic evidence references. Operational managers; governance leads
4. Findings — Composition Analysis Composition data tables, percentage composition by area and stream, benchmarking against 20:20:60. Graphs/charts of key findings. Comparison with previous audit where available. Operational managers; finance
5. Root Cause Analysis 5 Whys analysis for key/significant findings. Identifies systemic drivers behind non-conformances. Operational managers; quality leads
6. Recommendations and Action Plan SMART corrective actions for each finding. Prioritised by severity. Named owner, target date, verification method for each action. All stakeholders
7. Conclusions Overall assessment of waste management performance. Trend versus previous audits. Statement of immediate risks requiring urgent action. All stakeholders
Appendices Completed audit checklists, data recording sheets, photographic evidence, waste composition data tables, audit plan. Technical reviewers; regulators

Writing Clear, Actionable Findings

Each finding in your report should follow a consistent format: what was observed, where it was observed, why it is a non-conformance, and what standard it contravenes.

Finding Statement Template:
"During inspection of [area/location] on [date], it was found that [specific observation]. This does not conform with [standard/requirement reference]. This represents a [Critical/Major/Minor] finding because [impact/risk]."

Poor finding (avoid): "Sharps containers were not always properly managed in some areas."
Good finding: "During inspection of the Minor Injuries Unit treatment rooms on 14 February 2026, five sharps containers were observed to be filled to or beyond the manufacturer-indicated maximum fill line (¾ capacity). Containers filled beyond this level cannot be safely sealed, presenting a sharps injury risk to clinical staff and waste handlers. This does not conform with HTM 07-01 paragraph 4.36 and represents a Major finding."

SMART Corrective Actions

Every finding in your report must be matched with a corrective action. And every corrective action must be SMART:

Element What It Means in Audit Context Example
Specific Exactly what needs to be done — not vague directives "Update waste segregation training module to include tiger stripe bag category and the offensive/hygiene waste definition" — not "improve staff training"
Measurable How will you know the action has been completed? "Training module updated and signed off by Infection Control Lead; completion rate of ≥90% for target staff group by due date"
Achievable Realistic given resource and authority available to the named owner Assign to the person with the authority and capacity to actually implement the action — not to a front-line staff member who has no control over system change
Relevant Does the action address the root cause identified — not just the symptom? If root cause is absent tiger stripe bags in an area, the action must include procurement and placement of tiger stripe bags — not just communication
Time-bound A specific completion date — not "as soon as possible" "By 30 April 2026" — not "in the near future"

Timescales by Severity

Corrective action timescales should reflect the severity of the finding:

  • Critical findings: Immediate action required — within 24–72 hours. Examples: sharps containers overfilled (immediate replacement); clinical waste storage area unlocked (immediate rectification). Document the immediate action taken and a longer-term systemic fix.
  • Major findings: Action within 1–4 weeks. Examples: out-of-date waste transfer notes (renew within 2 weeks); training records showing significant gaps (training schedule in place within 2 weeks, completion within 4 weeks).
  • Minor findings: Action within 1–3 months. Examples: missing date on sealed bags in one area; minor labelling non-conformance.

Presenting Findings to Management

The written report is your formal record, but the presentation to management is often where the real decisions get made. How you frame and present findings significantly influences whether corrective actions are resourced and implemented.

Know Your Audience

Senior management are primarily motivated by risk (legal, financial, reputational) and resource. Frame your findings in these terms. "42% of clinical waste is mis-classified, costing an estimated £65,000 annually in avoidable disposal costs" is more compelling to a Director of Finance than "staff are not correctly using tiger stripe bags." Both statements describe the same situation — but only one speaks the language of your audience.

Include a clear financial quantification in your executive summary wherever possible. Even rough estimates, clearly labelled as such and based on transparent assumptions, are more persuasive than no financial dimension at all.

Lead With Risk, Follow With Improvement

Open with your critical and major findings — these require management attention and potentially immediate resource allocation. Follow with the improvement opportunity that the findings represent. Avoid leading with good news ("overall performance was reasonable") followed by problems — this structure allows management to focus on the positive and discount the findings.

Securing Ownership

Before leaving the management presentation, ensure that each corrective action in your plan has a named owner in the room who has accepted responsibility. Corrective actions without named owners are aspirations. The management meeting is the moment to secure that ownership — and to have it minuted.

Follow-Up Verification

The audit cycle is not complete when the report is written and presented. You have a responsibility to verify that corrective actions have been implemented and are effective. Build follow-up verification into your audit plan from the outset — specify the method and date for verification for each corrective action.

Verification may take the form of: a document review (confirming that updated training records exist); a targeted re-audit of the specific area or issue (for composition findings); or a physical inspection (confirming that a previously unlocked waste storage area is now secured).

Where corrective actions are not completed by their due date, escalate to the relevant manager and document the escalation. Persistent non-completion of audit corrective actions is itself a governance finding that should be reported at the appropriate level.

Key Takeaways

☐Quantify findings numerically — percentages and costs are more compelling and harder to dismiss than qualitative observations
☐Apply 5 Whys analysis to significant findings to identify root causes — corrective actions that address root causes produce sustained improvement
☐Avoid person-blaming conclusions — design corrective actions that change systems, not just individuals
☐Structure the report with an executive summary, scope, methodology, findings, root cause analysis, and SMART action plan
☐Write findings using a specific, evidence-linked format — observation, location, standard contravened, severity
☐SMART corrective actions require: named owner, specific action, measurable completion criteria, achievable scope, and a fixed due date
☐Critical findings require action within 24–72 hours; major within 1–4 weeks; minor within 1–3 months
☐Present findings to management in the language of risk and cost — and secure named ownership of corrective actions in the meeting

Reflection Questions

  1. Apply the 5 Whys technique to the following finding from your own (or a hypothetical) healthcare setting: "Waste transfer notes could not be located for three of the last twelve months." Follow the chain of causation until you reach a systemic root cause — and then design a corrective action that addresses that root cause.
  2. You have a composition audit finding showing 48% mis-segregation in yellow bags from the physiotherapy department. How would you present this finding to the Head of Physiotherapy — who is concerned that auditing is being used to criticise clinical staff — in a way that is honest, constructive, and likely to generate genuine engagement with the corrective action plan?
  3. Review the SMART corrective action table in this lesson. Take one corrective action from a previous audit you have been involved in (or design a hypothetical example) and test it against each of the five SMART criteria. What does it pass, and where does it fall short?

lessons.title: Case Study: A Full Audit Walkthrough
lessons.content:
Bringing It All Together: A Real Audit in Action

Everything you have learned in Modules 1 through 4 now comes together in this lesson. Rather than presenting principles in the abstract, we are going to work through a complete healthcare waste audit from planning to final report — walking through every decision, every finding, and every corrective action as it would actually unfold in practice.

The organisation we are auditing is Meadowfield Community Hospital — a fictional but realistic 120-bed community hospital providing general medicine, minor injuries, outpatient clinics, and dental services. I have designed this case study to reflect the kinds of findings that are genuinely typical in UK community healthcare settings: not catastrophic failures, but the accumulated effect of training gaps, procurement oversights, and documentation drift that characterises most organisations audited for the first time in several years.

Lesson Objectives: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to (1) recognise common waste management non-conformances across different clinical settings; (2) interpret composition audit data and calculate mis-segregation rates; (3) apply 5 Whys analysis to realistic findings; and (4) follow the structure of a complete audit report with corrective actions and accountability assignments.

Audit Overview: Meadowfield Community Hospital

Organisation: Meadowfield Community Hospital (fictional)

Audit Reference: MCH-WA-2026-01

Audit Type: Combined Compliance, Composition, and System Audit

Lead Auditor: Estates and Sustainability Manager

Audit Team: Lead Auditor; Infection Control Practitioner; Waste Management Officer (non-clinical area)

Areas Audited: (1) Outpatient Clinic; (2) Minor Injuries Unit (MIU); (3) Dental Suite

Waste Streams Examined: Yellow bags (clinical), tiger stripe bags (offensive/hygiene), yellow-lidded sharps containers, domestic/general waste, dental-specific waste streams

Documentation Review Period: January 2024 – February 2026 (24 months)

Audit Dates: 10–12 February 2026

Standards Referenced: EPA 1990 s.34; HTM 07-01 (2013, updated 2022 guidance); Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005; ISO 14001:2015

Area 1: Outpatient Clinic

Physical Inspection Observations

The outpatient clinic runs Monday to Friday, seeing approximately 80–100 patients per day across general medicine, wound care, and chronic disease management clinics. The clinical waste infrastructure consists of yellow bags at each treatment bay, a shared tiger stripe bag receptacle near the nurses' station, and one sharps container per treatment room.

During physical inspection, the audit team observed:

  • Tiger stripe bags were present in the clinic corridor but absent from three out of six individual treatment bays. Staff confirmed that tiger stripe bags had been out of stock for approximately three weeks following a procurement delay.
  • One sharps container in Treatment Bay 4 was observed to be filled beyond the ¾ maximum fill line indicator — it had been sealed but the seal was not fully engaged (a safety risk during handling and transport).
  • Yellow bags in treatment bays were correctly colour-coded and labelled with the clinic name. However, four of six bags had no date of seal recorded — a minor non-conformance with HTM 07-01 paragraph 6.12.
  • The waste storage room adjacent to the clinic was unlocked and unsecured at 14:30 on 10 February — during a period when waste had been placed in the room awaiting collection.

Composition Audit — Outpatient Clinic Yellow Bags

Sample: 4 yellow bags, gross weights 1.6kg, 2.1kg, 1.9kg, 1.7kg. Total gross weight: 7.3kg

Category Total Weight % of Total Notes
Genuinely infectious clinical waste 2.85kg 39.0% Contaminated dressings, blood-contaminated items
Offensive/hygiene waste (non-infectious) 3.07kg 42.1% Gloves, aprons, uncontaminated packaging — should be tiger stripe
Domestic/general waste 1.02kg 14.0% Paper, plastic packaging, food wrapper — should be domestic stream
Pharmaceutical waste 0.36kg 4.9% Topical cream tubes, empty blister packs — should be pharmaceutical waste
TOTAL 7.3kg 100%
61.0% Outpatient Clinic mis-segregation rate in yellow bags — items that should travel in alternative streams

Root Cause Analysis — Outpatient Clinic High Mis-Segregation Rate:

Why is 42% of yellow bag weight offensive waste? → Tiger stripe bags are unavailable in treatment bays. Why? → Stock ran out three weeks ago. Why? → No automatic re-order trigger when tiger stripe bag stock falls below minimum. Why? → Tiger stripe bags are managed through an ad-hoc ordering system rather than the standard consumables management system. Why? → Tiger stripe bags were introduced as a new product two years ago but were never formally added to the consumables inventory system. Root cause: Absence of tiger stripe bags in the consumables management system, meaning stock depletion triggers no automatic re-order.

Area 2: Minor Injuries Unit (MIU)

Physical Inspection Observations

The MIU operates 08:00–20:00 seven days a week. It sees a diverse case mix including lacerations, fractures, minor burns, and dressing changes. Waste generation is moderate-to-high, with a significant proportion of sharps waste from suturing and venepuncture procedures.

  • Tiger stripe bags were absent from MIU treatment bays — consistent with the outpatient clinic. Staff confirmed the same stock shortage.
  • Three wound care nurses were interviewed during the audit. When asked to describe the difference between yellow and tiger stripe bags, two of the three were unable to give a correct explanation of offensive/hygiene waste classification. One stated: "I just use the yellow bag for everything clinical — it feels safer."
  • Sharps containers in MIU treatment rooms were correctly positioned and appropriately filled. This was a positive finding.
  • Internal clinical waste collection records indicated that collection from the MIU storage area had occurred only twice in the past three weeks — the HTM 07-01 maximum storage period for Category B waste is 5 days, implying collections should occur at minimum every 5 days. The current pattern, suggesting 10-day intervals, represents a non-conformance.

Composition Audit — MIU Yellow Bags

Sample: 3 yellow bags, gross weights 2.4kg, 2.0kg, 2.2kg. Total gross weight: 6.6kg

Category Total Weight % of Total Notes
Genuinely infectious clinical waste 3.10kg 47.0% Suture materials with blood, contaminated wound care items
Offensive/hygiene waste 2.77kg 42.0% Gloves, aprons, packaging — should be tiger stripe
Domestic/general waste 0.73kg 11.0% Paper towels, uncontaminated packaging
TOTAL 6.6kg 100%
53.0% MIU mis-segregation rate — consistent with outpatient clinic, reinforcing that this is an organisation-wide issue

Area 3: Dental Suite

Physical Inspection Observations

The dental suite provides NHS and private dental services across four surgeries. Dental waste management is particularly complex due to the presence of regulated dental-specific waste streams: amalgam (mercury-containing), x-ray fixer solution (silver-bearing), lead foil from x-ray packaging, and plaster of Paris from impression work.

  • The audit team observed no amalgam waste containers in two of four dental surgeries. In Surgery 3, used amalgam capsules were found loose in a yellow clinical waste bag — a significant non-conformance. Amalgam is a hazardous waste (EWC code 18 01 10) and must be collected by a specialist dental waste contractor for mercury recovery, not incinerated.
  • In Surgery 2, a jar labelled "extracted teeth" contained two teeth with amalgam restorations mixed in with teeth without restorations. Amalgam-bearing extracted teeth require the same specialist disposal route as amalgam waste — separating them from non-amalgam extracted teeth had not been consistently practised.
  • All four dental surgeries were using yellow bags for general clinical waste. Tiger stripe bags were absent, consistent with the rest of the hospital.
  • Staff interview: Three of four dental nurses were asked to identify the correct bin for amalgam waste. None could identify the correct disposal route. One believed it should go in the yellow clinical bag; one was unsure; one had never received specific training on amalgam disposal.

Composition Audit — Dental Suite Yellow Bags

Sample: 3 yellow bags from dental surgeries, gross weights 1.2kg, 1.4kg, 1.1kg. Total gross weight: 3.7kg

Category Total Weight % of Total Notes
Genuinely infectious clinical waste 1.59kg 43.0% Contaminated swabs, impression trays in contact with blood
Offensive/hygiene waste 1.44kg 38.9% Gloves, bibs, uncontaminated packaging
Amalgam/dental hazardous waste 0.44kg 11.9% Used amalgam capsules, amalgam-bearing extracted teeth — CRITICAL: requires specialist disposal
Domestic waste 0.23kg 6.2% Paper, plastic packaging
TOTAL 3.7kg 100%

Critical Finding — Amalgam in Yellow Bags: The presence of amalgam waste in yellow bags designated for incineration represents a significant environmental and regulatory non-conformance. Incinerating amalgam releases mercury vapour — a hazardous air pollutant. This is a breach of the Hazardous Waste Regulations 2005 and potentially the Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016. The dental suite's contractor must be informed immediately that amalgam waste is reaching the incineration stream, and immediate corrective action is required.

System and Documentation Audit Findings

In addition to the physical and composition audit findings, the system and documentation review identified four significant issues:

Finding S1 — Waste Transfer Notes: Out of Date (CRITICAL)
A review of waste documentation revealed that the waste transfer notes (WTNs) for non-hazardous clinical waste have not been renewed since August 2024 — they are now 6 months overdue. WTNs for Category B clinical waste must be renewed at least annually under the Environmental Protection (Duty of Care) Regulations 1991. Current notes are legally deficient. This is a Critical finding requiring immediate rectification.

Finding S2 — Waste Management Policy: Not Reviewed Since 2019 (MAJOR)
The Meadowfield Community Hospital Waste Management Policy is dated March 2019 — over 6 years without formal review. The policy makes no reference to tiger stripe bags (introduced nationally post-2019), does not reflect the 2022 updated HTM 07-01 guidance notes, and names two individuals who left the organisation in 2021 and 2023. HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 requires policies to be reviewed at least every 2–3 years, or following significant regulatory changes. This represents a Major finding.

Finding S3 — Training Records: Bank Staff Excluded (MAJOR)
A review of waste management training records against establishment and bank staff lists revealed that 100% of substantive staff have completed waste awareness training within the last 18 months. However, there is no evidence that any of the hospital's 47 regular bank staff have received waste management training. Bank staff provide approximately 35% of clinical hours across the three audited areas. This is a Major finding: the training programme does not cover a significant portion of the workforce delivering clinical care.

Finding S4 — Amalgam Contractor Oversight: Not Verified (MAJOR)
The dental suite uses a specialist dental waste contractor for amalgam collection. A review of contractor documentation found that the contractor's Environment Agency hazardous waste carrier registration has not been verified or recorded since the contract was established in 2021. The current validity of the contractor's registration could not be confirmed during the audit. EPA 1990 s.34 requires waste producers to take all reasonable steps to ensure waste is transferred to an authorised person. Inability to verify contractor authorisation represents a duty of care weakness.

The Audit Report: Summary of All Findings

Finding Ref Area Finding Severity Standard
C1 Hospital-wide Tiger stripe bags out of stock in all three clinical areas for 3+ weeks Major HTM 07-01 Annex A; waste minimisation duty
C2 Outpatients / MIU 42% / 42% of yellow bag weight is offensive/hygiene waste — average mis-segregation rate 57% across audited areas Major HTM 07-01 Chapter 3 — correct segregation at source
C3 Dental Suite Amalgam waste present in yellow bags — incineration route; environmental and regulatory breach Critical Hazardous Waste Regs 2005 EWC 18 01 10; Env. Permitting Regs 2016
C4 Dental Suite Dental staff cannot identify correct disposal route for amalgam waste — 0 of 3 correctly identified Major HTM 07-01 Chapter 7 — training requirements
C5 MIU Clinical waste collection frequency below required standard — evidence of 10-day intervals vs. 5-day maximum Major HTM 07-01 para 5.16 — storage periods
C6 Outpatients Waste storage area found unlocked during audit — unsecured clinical waste Major HTM 07-01 Chapter 5 — storage requirements
C7 Outpatients Overfilled sharps container with improperly engaged seal Major HTM 07-01 para 4.36; UN3291 packaging requirements
S1 Hospital-wide Waste transfer notes 6 months overdue for renewal Critical EPA 1990 s.34; EP (Duty of Care) Regs 1991
S2 Hospital-wide Waste management policy not reviewed since 2019 — pre-dates tiger stripe bags and 2022 HTM updates Major HTM 07-01 Chapter 6 — policy review requirements
S3 Hospital-wide Bank staff (35% of clinical workforce) have no documented waste management training Major HTM 07-01 Chapter 7 — training requirements
S4 Dental Suite Specialist dental waste contractor authorisation not verified since 2021 Major EPA 1990 s.34 — duty of care; authorised carrier verification

The Corrective Action Plan

The following corrective action plan was agreed with Meadowfield management at the findings presentation on 19 February 2026. Each action is SMART: specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound.

Finding Ref Corrective Action Owner Due Date Verification Method
C3 (Critical) Immediately cease placing amalgam waste in yellow bags. Issue urgent instruction to all dental staff. Contact dental waste contractor for emergency amalgam container delivery within 48 hours. Notify current clinical waste contractor that amalgam may have entered incineration stream — obtain confirmation of handling. Document all actions. Dental Suite Manager / Estates Manager 14 February 2026 (48 hours from report) Written confirmation from contractor; immediate re-audit of dental bins within 7 days
S1 (Critical) Renew all waste transfer notes for clinical waste and hazardous waste streams immediately. Obtain updated documentation from current waste contractors. File and confirm receipt. Estates Manager 21 February 2026 Physical review of renewed WTN documentation by Lead Auditor
C1, C2 Emergency procurement of tiger stripe bags for all three clinical areas. Add tiger stripe bags to the hospital's standard consumables inventory management system with a minimum stock threshold and auto-reorder trigger. Update requisition procedure. Procurement Manager / Estates Manager Emergency stock: 17 February 2026. Inventory system update: 28 February 2026 Stock delivery confirmation; inventory system screenshot showing tiger stripe bags as managed item
C4, S3 Commission updated waste management training module incorporating: (1) tiger stripe bag category and offensive waste definition; (2) dental amalgam disposal procedures (for dental staff).
